entries.title: GLP-1 receptor agonists in stroke prevention: a narrative review on emerging therapeutic frontiers.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists may cut ischemic stroke risk by up to 39%
entries.summary: This narrative review synthesizes evidence on GLP-1 receptor agonists for stroke prevention in type 2 diabetes. Long-acting agents like semaglutide show 15–39% reductions in nonfatal and ischemic stroke risk through anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and neuroprotective mechanisms.
keyFindings: Long-acting GLP-1RAs reduced nonfatal and ischemic stroke risk by 15–39% across major cardiovascular outcome trials
Cerebrovascular benefits appear independent of glycemic control and greatest in patients with preserved renal function
Short-acting exendin-based GLP-1RAs showed limited cerebrovascular benefit compared to long-acting agents
Protective mechanisms include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, neuroprotective, and endothelial-stabilizing effects
entries.limitations: As a narrative review, findings reflect synthesis of heterogeneous trials with inconsistent outcomes and limited data in non-diabetic or high-risk populations.
entries.context: This review consolidates emerging evidence that GLP-1RAs provide cerebrovascular protection beyond their established cardiovascular benefits in type 2 diabetes. The findings extend prior cardiovascular outcome trial data by highlighting stroke-specific risk reductions and mechanistic pathways.

entries.title: Three-year changes in renal function after switching from injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists to oral semaglutide in Japanese patients with type 2 diabetes: a retrospective cohort study.
entries.displayTitle: Switching to oral semaglutide slows kidney decline over 3 years
entries.summary: This retrospective cohort study of 36 Japanese type 2 diabetes patients examined renal outcomes 3 years after switching from injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists to oral semaglutide (7 mg/day), finding significant improvements in both albuminuria and eGFR decline rate.
keyFindings: Median UACR decreased significantly from 54.3 to 35.8 mg/g creatinine over 3 years post-switch.
Annual eGFR slope improved from -1.92 to -0.26 mL/min/1.73 m²/year after switching (difference: 1.67).
Improvements in eGFR slope were significantly associated with greater weight loss and lower baseline HbA1c.
HbA1c decreased by 0.33% and body weight by 3.50 kg, both sustained at 3 years.
entries.limitations: Very small sample (n=36), retrospective design without a control group limits causal inference and generalizability beyond the Japanese clinical setting.
entries.context: The FLOW trial established renoprotective effects of subcutaneous semaglutide; this study extends prior findings by suggesting oral semaglutide may offer similar long-term renal benefits in real-world clinical practice after switching from other injectable GLP-1RAs.

entries.title: Effectiveness and Safety of Semaglutide in Type 1 Diabetes: A Danish Nationwide Cohort Study (2018-2024).
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide cuts HbA1c in type 1 diabetes without raising DKA or hypoglycemia risk
entries.summary: This Danish nationwide cohort study (2018–2024) followed 879 adults with type 1 diabetes who initiated semaglutide, matched 1:4 to unexposed controls. Semaglutide reduced HbA1c by 5.7 mmol/mol (0.52%) over six months with no increased hospitalization for hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis.
keyFindings: HbA1c fell 5.7 mmol/mol (0.52%) in the first 6 months and remained stable thereafter in semaglutide users.
No increased hospitalization for hypoglycemia vs. matched controls (HR 0.64; 95% CI: 0.35–1.19).
No increased diabetic ketoacidosis hospitalization rate (HR 0.73; 95% CI: 0.34–1.57).
One-year adherence to semaglutide was only 50%, suggesting real-world tolerability challenges.
entries.limitations: Observational design precludes causal inference; residual confounding is possible, and hospitalization-based safety endpoints may underestimate milder adverse events.
entries.context: Semaglutide is unapproved for type 1 diabetes due to limited evidence and concerns about ketoacidosis risk. This large real-world study appears consistent with modest glycemic benefit while providing reassurance that hospitalization rates for key safety outcomes were not elevated.

entries.title: Atypical retrobulbar optic neuropathy after semaglutide escalation.
entries.displayTitle: Case report: atypical retrobulbar optic neuropathy after semaglutide dose escalation
entries.summary: A man in his early 30s developed acute bilateral visual dysfunction four weeks after semaglutide escalation to 1 mg/week, presenting with retrobulbar optic neuropathy atypical for both NAION and demyelinating optic neuritis. Visual function stabilized after semaglutide discontinuation but a left central scotoma persisted.
keyFindings: Bilateral asymmetric visual field defects emerged within 4 weeks of semaglutide escalation to 1 mg/week.
Markedly delayed visual evoked potentials and central scotoma in left eye; altitudinal defect in right eye.
Presentation was atypical for NAION and did not fully align with classical demyelinating optic neuritis.
Discontinuing semaglutide stabilized vision; no further deterioration on serial follow-up.
entries.limitations: Single case report with no control, making causality with semaglutide impossible to establish; spontaneous recovery or an undetected demyelinating etiology cannot be excluded.
entries.context: Pharmacovigilance signals have previously flagged an increased NAION incidence among semaglutide users; this case extends those concerns to an atypical retrobulbar pattern, suggesting GLP-1RA-associated optic neuropathy may present along a broader clinical spectrum than previously recognized.

entries.title: 15-PGDH inhibition promotes muscle repair and strength recovery during GLP-1 receptor agonist-induced weight loss.
entries.displayTitle: 15-PGDH inhibitor rescues semaglutide-impaired muscle repair in obese mice
entries.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
entries.summary: In an obese mouse model, semaglutide caused significant skeletal muscle mass loss and impaired post-injury myofiber regeneration. Co-treatment with a 15-PGDH inhibitor restored muscle stem cell function, myofiber growth, and strength without compromising semaglutide-induced weight loss.
keyFindings: Semaglutide alone caused significant skeletal muscle mass loss in high-fat diet obese mice.
Post-injury, obese mice showed pathological calcifications similar to those in Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.
Semaglutide reduced calcific remodeling but impaired regenerated myofiber size after injury.
15-PGDH inhibitor co-treatment rescued myofiber growth and enhanced muscle strength without reducing weight loss.
entries.limitations: Findings are from a mouse obesity model only; translation to humans is uncertain, and long-term safety of combined semaglutide plus 15-PGDH inhibitor treatment was not assessed.
entries.context: Muscle mass loss during GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy is an emerging concern; this study extends prior findings by demonstrating a mechanistic link to impaired muscle regeneration and offering 15-PGDH inhibition as a potential co-intervention to preserve muscle quality.

entries.title: Steady-state pharmacokinetics of oral semaglutide using semi-mechanistic pharmacokinetic modelling and population simulations.
entries.displayTitle: Semi-mechanistic PK model predicts oral semaglutide steady-state absorption
entries.journal: Xenobiotica; the fate of foreign compounds in biological systems
entries.summary: Researchers developed a semi-mechanistic pharmacokinetic model for oral semaglutide using published clinical data, characterizing how SNAC enhancer, dose, gastrointestinal permeability, and intestinal first-pass effects drive absorption. The validated model covers IV, single-dose, and multiple-dose oral regimens.
keyFindings: Oral semaglutide bioavailability is only 0.4–1% in the fasting state due to its large molecular structure.
SNAC permeation enhancer significantly influences gastric absorption and oral bioavailability of semaglutide.
Dose, SNAC concentration, GI permeability, and intestinal first-pass effect are key PK drivers identified by the model.
The validated model is intended to support PBPK development for formulation optimization, drug interactions, and special populations.
entries.limitations: The model relies entirely on published literature-derived parameters rather than original experimental data, which may limit its accuracy for novel populations or formulations.
entries.context: Oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) requires SNAC to overcome poor GI absorption. This model extends prior clinical PK findings by providing a semi-mechanistic framework that could inform formulation and drug-interaction research, consistent with known absorption challenges for large peptide molecules.

entries.title: Dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist tirzepatide ameliorates hepatic steatosis and inflammatory responses in a MASLD mouse model associated with the CCL2/CCR2 axis.
entries.displayTitle: Tirzepatide reduces liver fat and inflammation via CCL2/CCR2 pathway in MASLD mice
entries.summary: This mouse study examined tirzepatide (TZP) and semaglutide in a high-fat/high-fructose MASLD model. TZP improved metabolic markers and reduced hepatic steatosis and inflammation, with transcriptomic and proteomic profiling linking the effects to downregulation of the CCL2/CCR2 chemokine axis and PI3K-AKT signaling.
keyFindings: TZP improved fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, ALT/AST, and hepatosomatic index compared to untreated HFHFr mice.
TZP reduced hepatic pro-inflammatory mediators MCP-1, IL-1β, TNF-α, and GSDMD, with partial IL-10 restoration.
Integrated transcriptomics and proteomics identified chemokine signaling and PI3K-AKT pathways as key TZP-associated signatures.
Targeted validation confirmed TZP reduced CCL2/CCR2 axis components and decreased PI3K abundance and AKT phosphorylation.
entries.limitations: Small sample size (n=32 mice across four groups) and an exclusively male mouse model limit generalizability; causal mechanisms require further validation beyond association.
entries.context: Tirzepatide has previously shown hepatoprotective effects in clinical and preclinical settings; this study extends prior findings by providing molecular mechanistic insight, specifically implicating the CCL2/CCR2 chemokine axis and PI3K-AKT pathway as key mediators of its anti-steatotic and anti-inflammatory actions.

entries.title: MOTS-c: How a secreted mitochondrial microprotein may become a potential treatment for inflammatory lung diseases.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c shows promise as biomarker and therapy for lung diseases
entries.summary: This review examines MOTS-c, a mitochondrial microprotein, as a candidate biomarker and therapeutic target in acute and chronic inflammatory lung diseases, synthesizing experimental and clinical evidence on its roles in oxidative stress, inflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction.
keyFindings: Circulating MOTS-c levels are reduced in acute respiratory distress; exogenous MOTS-c attenuates lung injury in preclinical models.
Chronic respiratory diseases (COPD, sleep apnea, asthma) are characterized by decreased MOTS-c, reflecting mitochondrial dysfunction.
Remote ischemic preconditioning appears to exert protective pulmonary effects partly via MOTS-c release.
Preliminary data suggest elevated MOTS-c in lung cancer, possibly linked to NRF2-mediated antioxidant responses; requires validation.
entries.limitations: Conclusions rest on preliminary and heterogeneous preclinical and clinical data; well-designed multicenter translational trials are explicitly acknowledged as lacking.
entries.context: MOTS-c was initially characterized as a metabolic regulator. This review extends its known biology into respiratory medicine, consistent with emerging evidence that mitochondrial-derived peptides coordinate systemic stress responses across multiple organ systems.

entries.title: GAIP ASD Research Study
entries.displayTitle: Phase 1 glutathione plus stem cell trial launches in pediatric ASD
entries.summary: This Phase 1 randomized crossover trial in ~100 individuals aged 3+ with ASD evaluates safety and preliminary effects of umbilical cord blood-derived stem cells/exosomes combined with glutathione versus glutathione alone, using ATEC score change at 6 months as the primary endpoint.
keyFindings: Phase 1, randomized participant-blinded crossover design; ~100 participants aged 3+ with confirmed ASD diagnosis.
Active arm: three monthly IV AdiaVita infusions plus escalating IV and transdermal glutathione; control: saline placebo plus same glutathione regimen.
Primary endpoint: change in ATEC score at Month 6; safety follow-up extends to 12 months with DSMB oversight.
Funded via $12,000 participant payments; exploratory design not powered to prove efficacy; currently recruiting.
entries.limitations: This is an active trial registration only; no results are available, and the participant-funded structure raises significant conflict-of-interest concerns that should temper interpretation of future findings.
entries.context: Glutathione deficiency has been hypothesized as a contributing factor in ASD oxidative stress pathways. Prior small cord blood studies in pediatric ASD reported acceptable safety profiles, providing rationale for combining these interventions, though efficacy remains unestablished.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Change from Baseline in Total Autism Treatment Evaluation Checklist (ATEC) Score at Month 6

entries.title: MOTS-c preserves mitochondrial subpopulation bioenergetics and genome integrity to attenuate cardiac ischemia reperfusion injury.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c preserves mitochondrial function and reduces cardiac IR injury in rats
entries.summary: Using an isolated Langendorff rat heart model, researchers found that MOTS-c (53 µM) administered before or at reperfusion onset improved post-ischemic mechanical recovery, reduced oxidative stress, and partially preserved mitochondrial bioenergetics and mtDNA integrity following 30-min global ischemia and 60-min reperfusion.
keyFindings: MOTS-c improved post-ischemic cardiac mechanical recovery compared to untreated IR controls.
Treatment attenuated oxidative stress and partially preserved electron transport chain and dehydrogenase enzyme activities.
MOTS-c mitigated IR-induced reductions in mtDNA copy number and mitochondrial regulatory gene expression.
Protective effects were seen in both subsarcolemmal and interfibrillar mitochondrial subpopulations, though responses varied.
entries.limitations: Small sample size (n=6/group), isolated ex vivo heart model limits translational relevance, and underlying signaling mechanisms were not elucidated.
entries.context: MOTS-c is a mitochondrial-derived peptide previously linked to metabolic stress adaptation and cytoprotection. These findings extend prior work by demonstrating cardioprotective effects specifically within distinct mitochondrial subpopulations during ischemia-reperfusion, consistent with its known role in mitochondrial homeostasis.

entries.title: Dynamic modulation of LH secretion by continuous kisspeptin infusion in healthy men.
entries.displayTitle: Continuous kisspeptin-10 infusion raises LH 5–8-fold in healthy men
entries.summary: Three healthy men received a 24-hour continuous IV infusion of kisspeptin-10 (12.5 mcg/kg/h), producing a rapid 5–8-fold rise in LH that partially declined before infusion end, suggesting receptor desensitization with sustained non-pulsatile stimulation.
keyFindings: LH rose from baseline 2.8–3.8 mIU/mL to peak 17.5–30.6 mIU/mL within 12–20 hours of infusion.
LH subsequently declined 13–47% from peak by infusion end, settling at 10.7–22.8 mIU/mL.
FSH and testosterone also rose modestly in parallel with LH.
Findings suggest partial desensitization of the kisspeptin receptor during continuous non-pulsatile stimulation.
entries.limitations: Extremely small sample size (n=3 men) with no control arm severely limits generalizability and statistical conclusions.
entries.context: Continuous kisspeptin infusion dampens GnRH release in non-human primates; this small human study appears consistent with that pattern, extending prior primate findings to men and complementing bolus-dosing approaches used to probe GnRH pulse generation.

entries.title: SPI-1005 for the Treatment of Meniere's Disease (Open Label)
entries.displayTitle: Phase 3 open-label SPI-1005 trial recruiting for Meniere's Disease
entries.summary: This open-label Phase 3 trial evaluates the long-term safety of SPI-1005 (ebselen, 400 mg BID), a glutathione peroxidase mimetic, in 200 adults with probable or definite Meniere's Disease over 6 or 12 months of treatment.
keyFindings: Phase 3, open-label, single-arm safety study; n=200 adults aged 18–75 with Meniere's Disease.
Intervention: SPI-1005 400 mg BID for 6 or 12 months to assess chronic/intermittent use safety.
Primary endpoint: incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events.
Status: currently recruiting; no results posted.
entries.limitations: This is an active trial registration with no results posted; open-label design limits ability to assess efficacy or control for placebo effects.
entries.context: Ebselen mimics glutathione peroxidase activity and has shown promise in earlier-phase trials for hearing-related conditions including Meniere's Disease. This Phase 3 study extends evaluation to long-term safety in a chronic-use setting.

entries.title: Lysine-proline-valine peptide attenuates hepatic lipid accumulation through ROS-dependent regulation of the PPARγ pathway in HepG2 cells.
entries.displayTitle: KPV tripeptide reduces fat buildup in liver cells via ROS-PPAR pathway
entries.summary: This in vitro study examined whether KPV (Lysine-Proline-Valine), an endogenous tripeptide derived from α-MSH, could protect HepG2 hepatic cells from oleic acid-induced lipid accumulation. At 100 µg/mL, KPV significantly reduced lipid deposition and fatty acid synthase expression by dampening ROS generation and suppressing ERK, AKT/mTORC1, and PPARγ phosphorylation.
keyFindings: KPV (100 µg/mL) significantly attenuated oleic acid-induced lipid accumulation in HepG2 cells without cytotoxicity.
KPV reduced reactive oxygen species generation, thereby preventing extracellular signal-regulated kinase activation.
KPV downregulated AKT phosphorylation, leading to inhibition of mTORC1 phosphorylation under steatosis conditions.
KPV normalized fatty acid synthase expression via regulation of PPARγ phosphorylation, a key lipogenesis transcription factor.
entries.limitations: Study is limited to a single human hepatocellular carcinoma cell line (HepG2) with no animal or clinical validation, limiting translational relevance.
entries.context: KPV is best known for its anti-inflammatory properties, particularly in gut inflammation models. These findings extend prior work by demonstrating antioxidant and anti-lipogenic effects in hepatocytes, suggesting a broader metabolic role consistent with its parent molecule α-MSH's involvement in energy homeostasis.

entries.title: Semaglutide-associated Twiddler syndrome.
entries.displayTitle: Case report: semaglutide-linked 25 kg weight loss triggers life-threatening Twiddler syndrome
entries.summary: A 52-year-old woman with an ICD developed Twiddler syndrome after losing 25 kg on semaglutide, causing lead entanglement and device malfunction. Lead extraction was complicated by cardiac tamponade, requiring ICD reimplantation after recovery.
keyFindings: Rapid 25 kg weight loss on semaglutide increased ICD generator mobility, leading to Twiddler syndrome.
Lead entanglement caused right ventricular lead dysfunction and painful device mobility.
Lead extraction triggered cardiac tamponade, a potentially life-threatening complication.
Authors warn GLP-1 agonist users with cardiac implantable devices may face elevated Twiddler syndrome risk.
entries.limitations: Single case report; causality between semaglutide-induced weight loss and Twiddler syndrome cannot be definitively established from one patient.
entries.context: Twiddler syndrome is a rare mechanical ICD/pacemaker complication; this case appears to be among the first linking GLP-1 receptor agonist-induced weight loss to increased generator pocket laxity as a precipitating mechanism.

entries.title: Semaglutide use for hypothalamic obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus after suprasellar germinoma treatment.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide cuts weight ~14% in hypothalamic obesity case
entries.summary: A 38-year-old woman with hypothalamic obesity (BMI 51.4) and type 2 diabetes following suprasellar germinoma treatment lost approximately 14% of body weight within 3 months after starting semaglutide (0.25–0.5 mg/week), with improved glycemic control and preserved muscle mass.
keyFindings: Weight dropped from 131 kg to 112.1 kg (~14% reduction) within 3 months of semaglutide initiation.
Appetite markedly decreased after semaglutide titration from 0.25 to 0.5 mg/week.
Glycemic control was optimized alongside weight loss.
Body composition showed fat mass reduction with relative preservation of muscle mass.
entries.limitations: Single case report with only 3-month follow-up; no control condition; findings may not generalize to all hypothalamic obesity etiologies.
entries.context: Hypothalamic obesity is notoriously resistant to standard interventions; GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide appear promising for this condition. This case extends limited prior evidence suggesting GLP-1 agonists may help override dysregulated hypothalamic appetite signaling.

entries.title: [Advances in Anti-Dementia Therapies in Older Adults].
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide fails in dementia trials; amyloid antibodies show promise
entries.summary: This narrative review covers recent advances in anti-dementia pharmacotherapy and non-pharmacological interventions, noting that GLP-1 analogs including semaglutide failed in recent trials for neurodegeneration, while amyloid-targeting antibodies lecanemab and donanemab show disease-modifying potential in early Alzheimer's disease.
keyFindings: Semaglutide and other GLP-1 analogs failed in recent clinical trials for neurodegenerative dementia.
Lecanemab and donanemab reduce amyloid plaques and slow cognitive decline in MCI and early Alzheimer's disease.
Amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA) complicate anti-amyloid antibody use, especially in APOE4 homozygotes.
Moderate exercise (150 min/week) and multimodal lifestyle interventions improve cognition in MCI and dementia.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review, it does not systematically synthesize evidence; details of the semaglutide trial failures (e.g., sample sizes, endpoints) are not provided in the abstract.
entries.context: Semaglutide had been a candidate for Alzheimer's disease repurposing given metabolic and neuroprotective hypotheses; this review's report of trial failure is a notable setback consistent with broader challenges of repurposing metabolic agents for neurodegeneration.

entries.title: Targeting Inflammation and Fibrosis in Lipedema: The Potential Role of Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonist Therapies.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 receptor agonists show early promise in lipedema management
entries.journal: Dermatologic surgery : official publication for American Society for Dermatologic Surgery [et al.]
entries.summary: This narrative review evaluated the potential role of GLP-1 receptor agonists, including tirzepatide, in managing lipedema. Only 13 publications were identified, with one small uncontrolled case series of 5 patients showing pain and limb volume improvements with exenatide.
keyFindings: Only 13 publications found; just 2 specifically evaluated GLP-1 RAs in lipedema.
One case series of 5 patients on exenatide reported improvements in pain and limb volume.
Tirzepatide may influence inflammatory and fibrotic pathways relevant to lipedema.
GLP-1 RAs not yet proven to directly alter lipedema progression; may serve as adjunctive therapy.
entries.limitations: Evidence base is extremely sparse; the only direct patient data comes from a single uncontrolled case series of just 5 patients, precluding any efficacy conclusions.
entries.context: Lipedema lacks effective medical therapies, with liposuction remaining standard of care. This review extends emerging interest in GLP-1 RAs beyond metabolic disease into fibroinflammatory fat disorders, consistent with their known anti-inflammatory and weight-reduction effects.

entries.title: The Role of Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists in Hair Loss: Clinical Evidence and Proposed Mechanisms.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists linked to hair loss, but may help inflammatory alopecia
entries.journal: Dermatologic surgery : official publication for American Society for Dermatologic Surgery [et al.]
entries.summary: This scoping review evaluated the association between GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, tirzepatide) and alopecia using PubMed literature through April 2026. Evidence suggests these drugs are associated with telogen effluvium and androgenic alopecia, while potentially improving inflammatory alopecia in metabolically dysfunctional patients.
keyFindings: GLP-1RAs are associated with telogen effluvium and androgenic alopecia, especially with tirzepatide and semaglutide.
Hair loss risk appears higher with longer use, greater weight loss, and higher doses.
Proposed mechanisms include weight loss-related changes, altered dermal white adipose tissue, and hormonal influences.
Smaller body of evidence suggests GLP-1RAs may improve inflammatory alopecia in patients with metabolic dysfunction.
entries.limitations: Evidence is conflicting and largely observational; causal mechanisms remain unclear and relative contributions of proposed pathways have not been established.
entries.context: As GLP-1 receptor agonists expand beyond diabetes into weight management, dermatologic side effects are increasingly reported. This review extends prior safety signals by systematically cataloguing alopecia associations and proposing mechanistic explanations, consistent with emerging pharmacovigilance concerns.

entries.title: GLPs Significantly Decrease the Risk of Postoperative Surgical Complications: A TriNetX Retrospective Cohort Study.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide and tirzepatide linked to fewer post-surgical complications
entries.journal: Dermatologic surgery : official publication for American Society for Dermatologic Surgery [et al.]
entries.summary: This retrospective cohort study using the TriNetX database evaluated GLP-1 receptor agonist use in patients undergoing Mohs surgery or wide local excisions for skin cancer. GLP users showed significantly reduced one-month risk of wound infection, disruption, hematoma, and other postoperative inflammatory complications, with semaglutide and tirzepatide showing the greatest reductions.
keyFindings: GLP-1 receptor agonist use was associated with significantly reduced risk of infection after a procedure.
Wound disruption, hematoma, skin/subcutaneous tissue infection, and other complications were all significantly reduced.
Semaglutide and tirzepatide demonstrated the greatest individual reductions in postoperative complications among all GLPs studied.
Findings support perioperative safety and a potential wound-healing benefit of GLP-1 agonists in dermatologic surgery.
entries.limitations: Retrospective design limits causal inference; residual confounding from unmeasured variables (e.g., diabetes control, obesity severity) may influence results despite demographic and comorbidity adjustment.
entries.context: GLP-1 receptor agonists have previously shown anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties in preclinical models. This study extends those findings to a clinical surgical setting, suggesting semaglutide and tirzepatide may confer meaningful perioperative benefits in skin cancer surgery patients.

entries.title: Cagrilintide and CagriSema for weight reduction and metabolic risk modification in overweight or obesity: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
entries.displayTitle: CagriSema cuts body weight ~6% in meta-analysis of 5,023 adults
entries.journal: Journal of diabetes and metabolic disorders
entries.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled four randomized controlled trials (n=5,023) evaluating cagrilintide alone or combined with semaglutide (CagriSema) in overweight/obese adults, finding both regimens produced clinically meaningful weight loss versus placebo with acceptable tolerability.
keyFindings: Cagrilintide alone reduced body weight by 6.08% versus placebo (95% CI: −8.02 to −4.14%).
CagriSema reduced body weight by 5.98% versus placebo (95% CI: −10.64 to −1.32%).
CagriSema significantly reduced waist circumference and HbA1c; cagrilintide alone modestly improved blood pressure.
Adverse events were more frequent with active treatment, but discontinuation rates were comparable to placebo.
entries.limitations: Only four trials were pooled, limiting statistical power; heterogeneity in trial duration and dosing may affect the precision of the combined estimates.
entries.context: Semaglutide is an established GLP-1 receptor agonist for weight management; combining it with cagrilintide, a long-acting amylin analogue, appears to extend cardiometabolic benefits. These findings are consistent with prior individual trials suggesting additive effects on weight and glycemic control.

entries.title: Kisspeptin-10 attenuates pulmonary arterial hypertension via restoration of mitochondrial function in pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells.
entries.displayTitle: Kisspeptin-10 reduces pulmonary arterial hypertension by restoring mitochondrial function
entries.summary: This study investigated Kisspeptin-10 in a mouse model of pulmonary arterial hypertension (SU5416/hypoxia), finding it significantly reduced right ventricular pressure, hypertrophy, and vascular remodeling by restoring mitochondrial function and normalizing excessive mitophagy in pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells.
keyFindings: Kisspeptin-10 via GPR54 markedly reduced right ventricular systolic pressure and hypertrophy in PAH mice.
Treatment restored mitochondrial membrane potential, ATP production, and cytochrome c oxidase activity in hypoxic PASMCs.
Kisspeptin-10 normalized elevated mitophagy proteins PINK1, Parkin, and FUNDC1 in PAH lungs.
In vitro, Kisspeptin-10 directly inhibited hypoxia-induced PASMC proliferation.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to a single mouse PAH model (SU5416/hypoxia); translational relevance to human PAH patients remains unestablished.
entries.context: Kisspeptin/GPR54 signaling is known primarily for reproductive regulation, but this study extends its role to pulmonary vascular biology, consistent with emerging evidence that kisspeptin has vasoprotective and anti-proliferative actions in non-reproductive tissues.

entries.title: Co-exposure to PS-NPs and HFPO-TA potentiates reproductive toxicity via ferroptosis and SLC1A5-Mediated glutamine deprivation in male mice.
entries.displayTitle: Glutathione supplementation reduces nanoplastic-PFAS reproductive toxicity in male mice
entries.summary: This study used male mice and Leydig cell models to examine combined toxicity of polystyrene nanoplastics and HFPO-TA (a PFAS compound). Co-exposure synergistically worsened ferroptosis, glutathione depletion, and spermatogenesis impairment, while glutathione supplementation markedly attenuated these effects.
keyFindings: Co-exposure to PS-NPs and HFPO-TA synergistically impaired testosterone synthesis and spermatogenesis in male mice.
HFPO-TA bound SLC1A5 glutamine transporter, suppressing glutamine uptake and blocking glutathione biosynthesis.
Ferroptosis markers GPX4 (down) and ACSL4 (up) were worsened by co-exposure with PS-NPs.
Glutathione supplementation and ferrostatin-1 both markedly attenuated co-exposure toxicity in Leydig cells.
entries.limitations: Findings rely on a mouse model and in vitro Leydig cells; direct translation to human reproductive health under real-world environmental exposure levels remains unestablished.
entries.context: Glutathione's role in protecting against ferroptosis via GPX4 is well established. This study extends prior findings by linking environmental co-contaminant-induced glutathione depletion to male reproductive toxicity through the SLC1A5 transporter axis.

entries.title: Efficacy and safety of oral semaglutide 14 mg (flexible dose) in early-stage symptomatic Alzheimer's disease (evoke and evoke+): two phase 3, randomised, placebo-controlled trials.
entries.displayTitle: Oral semaglutide fails to slow Alzheimer's progression in phase 3 trials
entries.summary: Two large phase 3 RCTs (evoke and evoke+) tested oral semaglutide 14 mg vs. placebo over 104 weeks in 3,808 adults with amyloid-confirmed early Alzheimer's disease. Semaglutide showed no significant difference from placebo on the primary cognitive/functional endpoint (CDR-SB), and both trials were discontinued due to negative outcomes.
keyFindings: No significant difference in CDR-SB change at week 104: estimated difference −0.08 (95% CI −0.35 to 0.20, p=0.57) in evoke and 0.10 (−0.17 to 0.38, p=0.46) in evoke+.
3,808 participants randomized across 566 sites in 40 countries; mean age 72.2 years, mean baseline CDR-SB 3.7.
Treatment-emergent adverse events occurred in 91.2% of semaglutide recipients vs. 84.8% of placebo recipients.
Safety and tolerability profile of semaglutide was consistent with its established profile in other indications.
entries.limitations: Both trials were industry-funded (Novo Nordisk), and the very small proportion of evoke+ participants with small vessel pathology (2.8%) limits conclusions for that subgroup; also, the flexible-dose design may have limited drug exposure.
entries.context: Prior observational and animal studies suggested GLP-1 receptor agonists might reduce dementia risk, motivating these trials. The evoke/evoke+ results contradict that hypothesis for early symptomatic Alzheimer's disease, extending prior work by providing the first large-scale phase 3 evidence against clinical benefit in this population.

entries.title: GLP-1 receptor agonists for weight management and potential thromboembolic risk reduction in high risk population with cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular disease: A systematic review.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists cut cardiovascular risk but thromboembolic evidence remains limited
entries.summary: This systematic review (8 studies, PRISMA 2020) examined GLP-1 receptor agonists—including semaglutide—for weight loss and thromboembolic risk reduction in high-risk populations. GLP-1RAs produced significant weight loss and reduced major cardiovascular events, but direct evidence for venous thromboembolism reduction was sparse and non-significant.
keyFindings: Semaglutide achieved up to 15.2% weight loss vs 2.6% in controls (p < 0.0001).
Cardiovascular outcome trials showed hazard ratios of 0.80 and 0.74 for major cardiovascular events.
Direct VTE data was limited; one study showed fewer events (1 vs 3) without statistical significance.
Serious adverse events were similar or lower in GLP-1RA groups (33.4% vs 36.4%).
entries.limitations: Only 8 studies were included, direct VTE reporting was minimal across trials, and no meta-analysis was performed, limiting quantitative synthesis.
entries.context: Consistent with established evidence that semaglutide and other GLP-1RAs reduce weight and cardiovascular risk; this review extends prior findings by attempting to characterize thromboembolic outcomes, an area where data appear insufficient to draw firm conclusions.

entries.title: Transcriptomics of S3 segment in mice: response to type 1 diabetes, SGLT1/2 inhibition, or GLP1 receptor agonism.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide restores 25% of diabetic kidney gene changes in mice
entries.journal: American journal of physiology. Renal physiology
entries.summary: This mouse study used laser-capture transcriptomics to examine gene expression in kidney S3 segments from diabetic Akita mice treated with SGLT2 inhibitor dapagliflozin, SGLT1 knockout, or GLP1R agonist semaglutide. Semaglutide restored the most differentially expressed genes (25%) and reduced cellular stress and proliferation pathways in diabetic kidneys.
keyFindings: Semaglutide restored 25% of diabetes-induced differentially expressed genes in kidney S3 segments, outperforming SGLT2i (12%) and Sglt1-KO (18%).
GLP1R agonism reduced cellular stress response and proliferation in diabetic S3 segments, associated with restored kidney SGLT1 membrane expression.
SGLT2i and diabetes shared ~34% overlapping gene direction changes; combined SGLT2i/Sglt1-KO was not synergistic.
A subset of diabetes-sensitive genes unresponsive to all three interventions was identified as potential new therapeutic targets.
entries.limitations: Study used only male mice of a single diabetic strain (Akita), limiting generalizability to females, other diabetes models, and humans.
entries.context: GLP1R agonists are known to provide renoprotective effects in diabetic kidney disease; these findings extend prior work by identifying specific transcriptomic pathways in the S3 proximal tubule segment that semaglutide modulates, consistent with stress-reduction and metabolic restoration mechanisms.

entries.title: Protective effects of BPC 157 in rats with experimentally induced lower extremity ischemia-reperfusion injury.
entries.displayTitle: BPC-157 reduces oxidative stress and apoptosis in rat limb ischemia-reperfusion
entries.summary: This rat study evaluated BPC-157 (20 µg/kg IP) in a lower limb ischemia-reperfusion model using aortic clamping. BPC-157 significantly reduced oxidative stress markers, downregulated pro-apoptotic genes, lowered IL-6 inflammation, and partially restored VEGF expression and muscle architecture.
keyFindings: BPC-157 reduced MDA and TOS while restoring SOD and TAS antioxidant levels after I/R injury.
Pro-apoptotic markers p53, Bax, and Caspase-3 were downregulated; Bcl-2 was significantly upregulated by BPC-157.
IL-6 and Caspase-3 immunoreactivity were reduced, and VEGF expression was partially restored in treated rats.
Histology confirmed improved skeletal muscle architecture and reduced collagen deposition with BPC-157 treatment.
entries.limitations: Very small sample size (n=6 per group) and a single dose tested; no dose-response data and no clinical translation established.
entries.context: These findings are consistent with prior research showing BPC-157's cytoprotective effects across multiple tissues, extending its known anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, and angiogenic properties to peripheral limb ischemia-reperfusion injury.

entries.title: Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists and Risk of Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide linked to 2.5x higher NAION risk in meta-analysis
entries.summary: This systematic review and Bayesian meta-analysis pooled 5 observational studies (1.59 million patients) to assess whether GLP-1 receptor agonists, particularly semaglutide, increase risk of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION). Semaglutide users had a 2.52-fold higher NAION risk versus non-GLP-1RA users, with 99.9% posterior probability of increased risk.
keyFindings: Semaglutide associated with RR 2.52 (95% CrI 1.56–4.72) for NAION vs. non-GLP-1RA users.
Association was stronger in diabetic patients: RR 2.41 (95% CrI 1.57–4.10), P(RR>1) 99.9%.
Incidence of NAION among semaglutide users was 118 per 100,000 (95% CrI 32–451).
Leave-one-out sensitivity analyses consistently supported the primary findings.
entries.limitations: Evidence certainty is rated only low-to-moderate; all included studies are observational, precluding causal inference, and residual confounding cannot be excluded.
entries.context: Prior individual observational studies reported conflicting evidence on GLP-1RA-associated NAION risk. This meta-analysis extends those findings by pooling the largest available evidence base, suggesting a consistent and statistically robust signal, though certainty of evidence is rated only low-to-moderate.

entries.title: Middle-aged mice treated with GHK-Cu peptide administered intraperitoneally or intranasally show behavioral rescue but divergent hippocampal aging programs.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu improves memory in aged mice via divergent molecular pathways by delivery route
entries.summary: Aged C57BL/6J mice (20–21 months) received GHK-Cu either intraperitoneally (5 days) or intranasally (8 weeks); both routes improved hippocampal-dependent spatial learning, but triggered distinct molecular programs — intranasal suppressed oxidative phosphorylation and mTOR signaling, while intraperitoneal activated DNA repair and stress-response pathways.
keyFindings: Intranasal GHK-Cu improved escape latency across multiple trials in both sexes; intraperitoneal produced only transient improvement in males.
Intranasal treatment reduced GFAP in both sexes and increased synaptophysin in females; intraperitoneal reduced TGF-β, GFAP, and MCP-1 in males.
Intranasal GHK-Cu strongly suppressed oxidative phosphorylation (NES −5.44 males, −4.20 females) and MYC target pathways (FDR < 0.0001).
Intraperitoneal GHK-Cu activated oxidative phosphorylation (NES 4.97), DNA repair (NES 5.58), and MYC targets — indicating acute stress-response engagement.
entries.limitations: This is a preprint study, so findings have not yet undergone full peer review; results are limited to a single inbred mouse strain and may not generalize to human aging.
entries.context: GHK-Cu is an endogenous copper-binding peptide known to decline with age and previously associated with anti-inflammatory and regenerative effects. This study extends prior findings by demonstrating that delivery route and exposure duration are critical determinants of the molecular mechanisms underlying cognitive benefit.

entries.title: Benchmarking size-exclusion chromatography columns for the analysis of therapeutic peptides and model oligonucleotides.
entries.displayTitle: SEC column benchmarking for semaglutide and peptide analysis optimized
entries.summary: Eleven size-exclusion chromatography columns were systematically evaluated for analyzing therapeutic peptides, including semaglutide and liraglutide, under varied mobile phase conditions. A phosphate-based mobile phase with 20% isopropanol was identified as optimal for hydrophobic peptides like semaglutide, balancing chromatographic efficiency with preservation of non-covalent aggregates.
keyFindings: A prototype SEC column achieved a reduced plate height near 1, markedly better than typical SEC performance (h = 2–3).
30% acetonitrile with 0.1% TFA provided best performance for linear, macrocyclic, disulfide-constrained, and lipid-conjugated peptides.
For semaglutide and liraglutide, phosphate mobile phase with 20% isopropanol was proposed to limit denaturation and characterize aggregates.
Columns with pore sizes ≥100 Å performed best; UP-SW2000, Biozen dSEC-1, and BioCore SEC-120 yielded top results.
entries.limitations: This is a purely analytical/chromatographic benchmarking study with no biological model; findings are limited to in vitro chromatographic performance and do not address pharmacological or clinical relevance.
entries.context: Semaglutide's hydrophobic, lipid-conjugated structure poses known analytical challenges. This study extends prior SEC methodology work by systematically identifying column and mobile phase conditions that preserve semaglutide's native aggregation state during characterization.

entries.title: Early Adjuvant Treatment with Semaglutide After Sleeve Gastrectomy in Patients with Class II-III Obesity: A Prospective Non-Randomized Controlled Study.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide after sleeve gastrectomy boosts 12-month weight loss by ~4%
entries.summary: This prospective non-randomized controlled study of 103 patients with class II-III obesity examined early adjunctive semaglutide (months 1–6 post-op) after laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy. At 12 months, the semaglutide group achieved significantly greater total weight loss (35.1% vs. 30.7%) and excess weight loss (86.4% vs. 76.9%) compared to surgery alone.
keyFindings: %TWL at 12 months was significantly greater with semaglutide: -35.1% vs. -30.7% (P=0.002).
%EWL at 12 months favored semaglutide: 86.4% vs. 76.9% (P=0.026).
At 6 months, %TWL was also significantly greater in the semaglutide group (P<0.001).
Exploratory analyses suggested greater lean mass preservation and visceral fat reduction with semaglutide.
entries.limitations: Non-randomized, single-center design with only 103 participants limits causal inference and generalizability; propensity score adjustment partially but not fully addresses baseline confounding.
entries.context: Combining GLP-1 receptor agonists with bariatric surgery is an emerging strategy; this study extends prior hypothesis-generating work by showing early short-term adjunctive semaglutide appears to amplify weight loss outcomes after sleeve gastrectomy.

entries.title: SPI-1005 in Adults Receiving Cochlear Implant
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 trial tests SPI-1005 to protect hearing after cochlear implant
entries.summary: This Phase 2 randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluates SPI-1005 (ebselen, a glutathione peroxidase mimetic) in 40 adults receiving a MED-EL cochlear implant, starting 2 days before surgery and continuing for 6 months, to assess safety and effects on hearing, tinnitus, and vertigo outcomes.
keyFindings: Phase 2 RCT, n=40, randomized to SPI-1005 or placebo for 6 months perioperatively.
Primary endpoint: incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (safety/tolerability focus).
Secondary endpoints include hearing thresholds, word recognition, speech discrimination, tinnitus, and vertigo.
Currently recruiting adults with moderate-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss scheduled for cochlear implantation.
entries.limitations: This is an active trial registration with no results posted; no conclusions about efficacy or safety can be drawn at this stage.
entries.context: SPI-1005 (ebselen) mimics glutathione peroxidase to reduce oxidative cochlear injury. Prior Sound Pharmaceuticals trials explored ebselen for noise-induced and drug-induced hearing loss; this is among the first trials targeting cochlear implant-related trauma.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Number of Participants with Treatment Emergent Adverse Events (TEAE)

entries.title: Semaglutide versus placebo in individuals with poor weight loss after bariatric surgery: a double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide cuts post-bariatric weight by 18% vs. placebo gain in RCT
entries.summary: This 68-week double-blind RCT (BARI-STEP) tested semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly in 70 adults with suboptimal weight loss after gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy. Semaglutide produced an 18% body weight reduction versus a 0.4% gain with placebo, a difference of ~19 percentage points.
keyFindings: Mean weight loss was 18.0% with semaglutide vs. +0.4% weight gain with placebo at 68 weeks.
Adjusted treatment difference was −19.18% (95% CI −23.4 to −14.8; P < 0.001).
Semaglutide also improved metabolic parameters and quality of life versus placebo.
Adverse events matched semaglutide's known safety profile; no treatment-related deaths occurred.
entries.limitations: Relatively small sample (n = 70; ITT n = 63) and single-trial evidence; generalizability may be limited given 83% female enrollment and single-center or limited-site design.
entries.context: Semaglutide 2.4 mg is established for obesity management in non-surgical populations; this trial extends prior findings by demonstrating substantial efficacy and acceptable safety specifically in patients with suboptimal bariatric surgery outcomes, a population with limited pharmacological options.

entries.title: Semaglutide drives weight loss through cAMP-dependent mechanisms in GLP1R-expressing hindbrain neurons.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide drives weight loss via cAMP signaling in hindbrain neurons
entries.summary: This study examined the intracellular signaling mechanisms by which semaglutide drives weight loss in GLP1R-expressing neurons of the area postrema (AP) in mice. Semaglutide activated both Gs- and Gq-dependent pathways, with cAMP signaling through Gs being essential — disrupting it abolished semaglutide-induced weight loss.
keyFindings: Semaglutide engages both Gs- and Gq-dependent signaling in area postrema GLP1R-expressing neurons.
Semaglutide drives graded cAMP increases in AP neurons via the Gs pathway.
PDE4 inhibition enhances and sustains semaglutide-induced cAMP responses in AP neurons.
Disrupting Gs or cAMP signaling in AP neurons abolishes semaglutide-induced weight loss and brain-wide activation.
entries.limitations: Mechanistic findings are preclinical; direct translation of specific intracellular signaling pathways to human obesity therapy remains to be established.
entries.context: Prior work established GLP1R agonists act centrally to suppress appetite, with the area postrema as a key brain site. This study extends those findings by systematically mapping the intracellular signaling architecture — specifically cAMP and calcium — through which semaglutide exerts its weight-loss effects.

entries.title: Elevated Serum Nardilysin Is Inversely Associated with Cardiovascular Disease in Kidney Transplant Recipients.
entries.displayTitle: Higher MOTS-c and nardilysin levels found in kidney transplant recipients
entries.summary: This observational cohort study measured serum MOTS-c and nardilysin (NRDc) in 118 kidney transplant recipients and 32 controls, finding both peptides significantly elevated in transplant patients, with higher NRDc independently associated with lower odds of prevalent cardiovascular disease.
keyFindings: MOTS-c was significantly higher in kidney transplant recipients vs. controls (178 vs. 142 ng/mL, P=0.014).
Higher ln-NRDc was independently associated with lower odds of cardiovascular disease (OR 0.59, 95% CI 0.34–0.97).
MOTS-c showed a non-significant trend toward lower allograft loss risk (adj. sHR 0.50, P=0.08).
Neither MOTS-c nor NRDc were associated with all-cause mortality or eGFR decline over ~29 months.
entries.limitations: Relatively small sample (118 KTRs, 32 controls), cross-sectional CV disease assessment, and short median follow-up (~29 months) limit causal inference and detection of incident events.
entries.context: MOTS-c is a mitochondria-derived peptide with emerging roles in metabolic and inflammatory regulation; this study extends prior findings into kidney transplant recipients, a population with high cardiometabolic burden, though MOTS-c showed no significant associations with cardiometabolic features here.

entries.title: Kisspeptin-10 regulates glycosaminoglycan and decorin content in human cardiac fibroblast cultures.
entries.displayTitle: Kisspeptin-10 boosts glycosaminoglycan and decorin in cardiac fibroblasts
entries.summary: This in vitro study examined kisspeptin-10 (KiSS-10) effects on extracellular matrix components in human cardiac fibroblast cultures. KiSS-10 dose-dependently increased glycosaminoglycan content and decorin secretion, with the glycosaminoglycan effect mediated via GPR54 and FAK signalling but not phospholipase C.
keyFindings: KiSS-10 caused a dose-dependent increase in glycosaminoglycan content in both cells and culture medium.
The glycosaminoglycan-stimulatory effect was abolished by GPR54 blocker (peptide 234) or FAK inhibitor 14.
Phospholipase C inhibition (D609) did not alter KiSS-10-induced glycosaminoglycan changes, excluding PLC involvement.
KiSS-10 increased decorin secretion but not decorin gene expression, suggesting post-transcriptional regulation.
entries.limitations: Cell line-based study with no in vivo validation; findings may not reflect complex cardiac fibroblast behaviour in intact myocardial tissue.
entries.context: Kisspeptin-10 is known primarily for neuroendocrine roles, but emerging evidence suggests cardiovascular functions. This study extends prior findings by identifying a novel role for KiSS-10 in cardiac ECM remodelling via GPR54/FAK signalling in human cardiac fibroblasts.

entries.title: BPC-157 as an Investigational Peptide Therapeutic: Biopharmaceutical Challenges, Formulation Strategies, and Translational Development Barriers.
entries.displayTitle: BPC-157 lacks validated formulations and pharmacokinetics, review finds
entries.summary: This narrative review critically evaluates BPC-157's pharmaceutical development status, concluding that despite 30+ years of preclinical data showing biological activity, no approved formulation, validated dosing regimen, or completed Phase II trial exists, and fundamental biopharmaceutical characterization remains absent.
keyFindings: Preclinical ADME studies confirm a sub-30-minute plasma half-life, yet biological effects persist for hours to days.
Intramuscular bioavailability ranges from 14–51% depending on species; human PK remains critically undercharacterized.
Available clinical data cover fewer than 30 subjects across three uncontrolled pilot studies with no standardized formulations.
No BCS classification, permeability data, or formal excipient compatibility studies exist for BPC-157.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review without formal quality assessment, it may be subject to selection bias, and conclusions reflect the authors' synthesis rather than a systematic evidence appraisal.
entries.context: BPC-157 has a substantial preclinical literature suggesting cytoprotective and regenerative effects across multiple organ systems. This review extends prior concerns about translational gaps by systematically framing them as pharmaceutical science deficiencies rather than questions of biological activity.

entries.title: Preconditioning and supplementation with glutathione ameliorates oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and restores metabolic imbalance in insulin-resistant myotubes.
entries.displayTitle: Glutathione supplementation partially restores metabolism in insulin-resistant muscle cells
entries.summary: This in vitro study tested whether glutathione (GSH) preconditioning and supplementation (0.1 and 1 mM) could counteract palmitic acid-induced insulin resistance in L6 skeletal myotubes. At 1 mM, GSH partially restored multiple metabolic imbalances including TCA cycle intermediates, nucleotides, amino acids, and phospholipid intermediates.
keyFindings: 1 mM GSH supplementation partially restored nucleotide levels (UMP, adenosine, AMP) disrupted by palmitic acid-induced lipotoxicity.
GSH at 1 mM rescued levels of GSH metabolism intermediates (glutamine, pyroglutamate) and TCA cycle intermediates (citrate).
Phospholipid intermediates (o-phosphocholine, choline), amino acids (proline, aspartate, threonine), and glycerol were partially restored by 1 mM GSH.
Metabolic perturbations were identified using 1H NMR spectroscopy, revealing broad pathway involvement in lipotoxicity.
entries.limitations: Results are limited to a single in vitro cell line (L6 myotubes); findings may not translate to in vivo or human insulin resistance without further validation.
entries.context: GSH deficiency is well-documented in insulin-resistant states, and dietary GSH supplementation has been associated with improved insulin sensitivity. This study extends prior findings by mapping specific metabolic pathways restored by GSH in a skeletal muscle lipotoxicity model.

entries.title: Body Composition and Adipose Tissue in HIV
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin for HIV lipodystrophy hepatic fat posts results after early termination
entries.summary: This Phase 4 single-arm trial evaluated daily tesamorelin 2 mg subcutaneous injection in HIV-infected patients with lipodystrophy and central adiposity, measuring hepatic lipid content, body composition, and metabolic parameters at 6 and 12 months. The trial was terminated early with only 6 enrolled participants.
keyFindings: Trial terminated early; only 6 of planned participants enrolled, severely limiting statistical power.
Primary endpoint: hepatic lipid content measured at baseline, 6, and 12 months post-treatment.
Secondary measures included adipose tissue biopsy, metabolic rate, and insulin sensitivity assessments.
Results have been posted despite early termination; conclusions should be interpreted with extreme caution given tiny sample.
entries.limitations: Trial was terminated after enrolling only 6 participants; posted results are underpowered and cannot support meaningful efficacy or safety conclusions.
entries.context: Tesamorelin is FDA-approved for reducing visceral adiposity in HIV lipodystrophy. This trial extended that work by specifically targeting hepatic lipid content, an understudied complication linked to cardiovascular risk in HIV patients with central adiposity.

entries.title: MOTS-c, a mitochondrial-derived peptide, ameliorates lysosomal membrane permeability and improves survival of soft tissue transplantation.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c improves ischemic flap survival by stabilizing lysosomal membranes
entries.summary: This study examined MOTS-c, a mitochondrial-derived peptide, in a rat ischemic skin flap model. MOTS-c improved blood flow, reduced lysosomal membrane permeabilization, suppressed endothelial pyroptosis, and enhanced autophagy via inhibition of PLA2G4A through the MAPK1-MAPK3-NF-κB signaling axis.
keyFindings: MOTS-c improved blood flow perfusion and enhanced angiogenesis and collagen remodeling in ischemic flaps.
MOTS-c reduced endothelial pyroptosis and lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) in ischemic tissue.
Mechanistically, MOTS-c suppressed PLA2G4A phosphorylation via the MAPK1-MAPK3-NF-κB cascade to restore homeostasis.
AAV-mediated PLA2G4A overexpression confirmed MOTS-c's protective effects operate through the PLA2G4A-LMP axis.
entries.limitations: Results are from an animal model only; clinical translation to human reconstructive surgery outcomes has not been evaluated.
entries.context: MOTS-c is known to reduce ROS and support mitochondrial function; this study extends prior findings by linking MOTS-c to lysosomal integrity and suggesting a novel therapeutic role in reconstructive surgery for ischemic tissue.

entries.title: Gingival Hyperpigmentation Treatment
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 trial tests glutathione injections for gingival hyperpigmentation
entries.summary: This Phase 2 RCT at Cairo University evaluates intradermal vitamin C plus glutathione injections versus conventional scalpel surgery for treating gingival hyperpigmentation in 30 adult patients with physiologic melanin-related pigmentation.
keyFindings: Phase 2, n=30, recruiting; comparing glutathione+vitamin C injection vs. surgical scalpel depigmentation.
Primary endpoint: degree of gingival pigmentation assessed by Takashi index and Gingival Pigmentation Index.
Glutathione mechanism: inhibits tyrosinase and melanosome transfer from melanocytes to keratinocytes.
Patients with Dummett-Bolden pigmentation scores 1–3 are eligible; systemically healthy adults only.
entries.limitations: This is an active trial registration with no results posted; evidence of efficacy or safety for this indication remains unpublished.
entries.context: Glutathione's antimelanogenic properties are established in dermatology for skin lightening; this trial is among the first to test its injectable use specifically in gingival tissue as an alternative to surgical depigmentation.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Degree of gingival pigmentation by Takashi index (Hanioka et al., 2005); Degree of gingival pigmentation by Gingival pigmentation index (Kumar et al., 2013)

entries.title: Tesamorelin as an Adjunct to Exercise for Improving Physical Function in HIV
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 trial tests tesamorelin plus exercise for physical function in aging HIV
entries.summary: This Phase 2 RCT is evaluating whether tesamorelin (1.28 mg/day SC) combined with a home-based exercise program improves physical function and muscle health in 100 sedentary, frail adults aged 50–80 living with HIV, compared to placebo plus exercise over 24 weeks.
keyFindings: Phase 2 RCT; n=100; randomized, placebo-controlled, 24-week intervention plus 24-week off-drug follow-up.
Primary endpoint: change in Repeated Chair Stand Time, a validated measure of lower-body functional strength.
All participants receive supervised home-based exercise; tesamorelin vs. placebo added on top.
Secondary outcomes include muscle mass/quality, quality of life, exercise adherence, and durability of effects post-treatment.
entries.limitations: Trial is actively recruiting; no results are available, and registry entries do not constitute evidence of efficacy or safety.
entries.context: Tesamorelin is FDA-approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy and has shown muscle mass benefits in prior studies, but its effects on physical performance and strength in aging, frail people with HIV have not been previously evaluated.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Change in Repeated Chair Stand Time

entries.title: Differing Presentations of Excess Visceral Abdominal Fat in People Living With HIV: Two Clinical Cases Highlighting Distinct Therapeutic Pathways With Tesamorelin and Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists.
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin targets visceral fat in HIV patients beyond BMI-based treatment
entries.journal: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America
entries.summary: Two case reports describe HIV-positive adults with excess visceral abdominal fat treated with tesamorelin and/or GLP-1 receptor agonists. Tesamorelin provided selective visceral fat reduction and improved lipid levels and well-being, particularly in a non-obese patient where weight-based therapies would be inadequate.
keyFindings: Non-obese HIV patient (BMI 27 kg/m²) treated with tesamorelin showed marked reductions in waist circumference and improved lipid levels.
Obese HIV patient on GLP-1 agonist had fluctuating weight and persistent visceral fat due to intermittent medication access.
Adding tesamorelin to the obese patient's regimen provided more targeted visceral adiposity reduction.
EVAF in HIV can persist regardless of BMI category, supporting fat-distribution-based treatment decisions.
entries.limitations: Only two cases reported, limiting generalizability; no controlled comparison or long-term follow-up data provided.
entries.context: Tesamorelin is FDA-approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy and has consistently shown selective visceral fat reduction in PLWH. These cases extend prior findings by illustrating its utility across different obesity phenotypes and as a complement to GLP-1 therapies.

entries.title: Effects of Semaglutide on Dumping Syndrome and Reactive Hypoglycemia After Bariatric Surgery: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide significantly reduces dumping syndrome and hypoglycemia post-bariatric surgery
entries.journal: Obesity reviews : an official journal of the International Association for the Study of Obesity
entries.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 7 studies (337 patients) evaluated semaglutide for dumping syndrome and reactive hypoglycemia after bariatric surgery, finding significant improvements in symptom scores, hypoglycemic episode frequency, quality of life, and glycemic variability.
keyFindings: Dumping symptom scores improved significantly (SMD -1.18; 95% CI -1.64 to -0.72; p<0.001).
Hypoglycemic episodes reduced significantly (SMD -0.85; 95% CI -1.22 to -0.48; p<0.001).
Quality of life improved (SMD 0.73) and glycemic variability decreased (SMD -0.71).
GI adverse events were common but rarely caused treatment discontinuation.
entries.limitations: Only 7 studies with 337 total patients were included, comprising heterogeneous study designs (RCTs, cohort studies, one case series), limiting the strength of conclusions.
entries.context: Semaglutide, as a GLP-1 receptor agonist, appears to modulate gastric emptying and insulin secretion, consistent with prior mechanistic rationale for its use in post-bariatric complications. These findings extend limited prior evidence by pooling data across RCTs and observational studies.

entries.title: Impact of semaglutide on liver fibrosis score in people with HIV: findings from the CNICS cohort.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide linked to reduced liver fibrosis scores in HIV patients
entries.summary: This observational cohort study examined 1,850 people with HIV initiating semaglutide across 10 US clinical sites. Semaglutide was associated with significant reductions in FIB-4 liver fibrosis scores, particularly in those with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis at baseline.
keyFindings: Semaglutide reduced FIB-4 scores in patients with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis (FIB-4 ≥1.3; ΔFIB-4: -0.11, P<0.01).
Greatest reductions occurred in advanced fibrosis group (FIB-4 >2.67; ΔFIB-4: -0.64, P<0.001).
No significant FIB-4 reduction was observed in patients with low baseline fibrosis (FIB-4 <1.3).
Mean treatment duration was 218 days; most patients had multiple cardiometabolic comorbidities.
entries.limitations: Pre/post quasi-experimental design without a randomized control group limits causal inference; confounding by indication and unmeasured variables cannot be excluded.
entries.context: GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide have shown promise for MASLD and liver fibrosis in the general population. These findings extend that evidence to people with HIV, a population with heightened metabolic and liver disease burden, appearing consistent with broader anti-fibrotic and metabolic benefits.

entries.title: CagriSema Versus Semaglutide Monotherapy or Placebo for Obesity: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials with GRADE Assessment.
entries.displayTitle: CagriSema beats semaglutide alone: 11 kg greater weight loss in meta-analysis
entries.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 4 RCTs (n=4,419) compared the dual therapy CagriSema (semaglutide + cagrilintide) against semaglutide monotherapy or placebo in people with obesity. CagriSema produced significantly greater weight loss, waist circumference reduction, and blood pressure lowering, but with more gastrointestinal adverse events.
keyFindings: CagriSema reduced percent weight loss with a large effect size (Cohen's d: -1.38; 95% CI: -1.84 to -0.91).
CagriSema achieved ~11 kg greater absolute weight loss and 9.41 cm greater waist circumference reduction vs. comparators.
Systolic blood pressure was reduced by an additional 7.06 mmHg with CagriSema.
Gastrointestinal adverse events were 32% more frequent with CagriSema (RR: 1.32).
entries.limitations: High heterogeneity across trials (I² = 94.8%) limits the precision of pooled estimates, and longer-term safety and efficacy data beyond current trial durations are lacking.
entries.context: Semaglutide is an established GLP-1 receptor agonist for obesity and type 2 diabetes. Adding cagrilintide (an amylin analog) appears to extend weight-loss efficacy beyond semaglutide alone, consistent with the hypothesis that dual receptor targeting enhances metabolic outcomes.

entries.title: Recombinant Human Thymosin Beta 4 for Injection(NL005) for Acute Myocardial Infarction
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 trial tests recombinant thymosin beta 4 for heart attack repair
entries.journal: ClinicalTrials.gov · Beijing Northland Biotech. Co., Ltd.
entries.summary: This Phase IIc multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluates two IV doses of recombinant human thymosin beta 4 (NL005) versus placebo in 189 anterior STEMI patients treated with primary PCI, measuring infarct size reduction by cardiac MRI at 90 days.
keyFindings: Phase IIc RCT; n=189 randomized 1:1:1 to NL005 10 µg/kg, 20 µg/kg, or placebo IV for 7 days post-PCI.
Primary endpoint: absolute and relative myocardial infarct size by CMR at Day 90.
Eligibility restricted to first anterior STEMI from LAD occlusion with no collateral supply.
Extended follow-up through Day 360; trial not yet recruiting as of May 2026.
entries.limitations: Trial has not yet begun recruiting; no results are available, and registration alone provides no evidence of clinical efficacy or safety.
entries.context: Thymosin beta 4 (TB-500) has shown cardioprotective and regenerative effects in preclinical myocardial injury models, promoting cardiomyocyte survival and angiogenesis. This is among the first formal Phase 2 trials evaluating a recombinant Tβ4 formulation in human AMI patients.

conditions: Acute Myocardial Infarction of Anterior Wall
Acute Myocardial Infarction With ST Elevation
Acute Myocardial Infarction of Left Ventricle
Acute Myocardial Infarction With ST Segment Elevation
trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Myocardial Infarct Size (absolute) at Day 90; Myocardial Infarct Size (relative) at Day 90

entries.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists Inhibit the Initiation of Toxic Amyloid-β42 Aggregation.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide and tirzepatide block toxic amyloid-β42 aggregation initiation
entries.journal: Journal of the American Chemical Society
entries.summary: Researchers tested five FDA-approved GLP-1 receptor agonists for direct effects on amyloid-β42 aggregation in vitro. Semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide all inhibited Aβ aggregation by targeting primary nucleation, with submicromolar IC values, providing molecular-level insight into potential Alzheimer's disease mechanisms.
keyFindings: Semaglutide and tirzepatide inhibited Aβ42 primary nucleation with submicromolar IC values.
Liraglutide showed the strongest primary nucleation suppression plus modest secondary nucleation inhibition.
Liraglutide significantly reduced fibril maturity, increased tortuosity, and attenuated fibril self-replication.
Semaglutide- and tirzepatide-associated fibrils had morphology and templating efficiency similar to unmodified fibrils.
entries.limitations: Results are from an in vitro cell-free system, so direct translation to physiologically relevant brain concentrations and in vivo Alzheimer's disease pathology remains uncertain.
entries.context: Preclinical and clinical evidence suggests GLP-1RAs protect against neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration; this study extends prior findings by demonstrating direct, mechanistically distinct inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation steps by specific GLP-1RAs.

entries.title: Esophageal Motility Patterns Are Not Impacted By Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonist Use.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists, including semaglutide, don't alter esophageal motility
entries.summary: This retrospective study compared high-resolution manometry results in 132 type 2 diabetes patients on vs. off GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide most common). No significant differences in esophageal motility diagnoses or GERD detection rates were found between groups.
keyFindings: 64.3% of GLP-1RA users had normal high-resolution manometry; no significant difference vs. controls (P=0.07).
Semaglutide was the most used GLP-1RA, accounting for 57.1% of cases.
No association found between GLP-1RA type or dose and esophageal motility diagnoses (P=0.51).
GERD detection rates were similar between GLP-1RA users and non-users (P=0.51).
entries.limitations: Retrospective design with a relatively small sample (42 cases) limits statistical power to detect modest motility differences.
entries.context: GLP-1RAs are known to delay gastric emptying, raising questions about broader GI motility effects. These findings appear reassuring, suggesting the delayed gastric emptying effect does not extend meaningfully to esophageal motility.

entries.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists and the Risk of Non-Arteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy: A Consensus Statement by the North American Neuro-Ophthalmology Society and the American Academy of Ophthalmology.
entries.displayTitle: Expert consensus: semaglutide may carry small NAION risk
entries.summary: The North American Neuro-Ophthalmology Society and American Academy of Ophthalmology issued a consensus statement on the possible link between GLP-1 receptor agonists (including semaglutide) and non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION), concluding that current evidence suggests a small possible increased risk but overall magnitude remains low.
keyFindings: Most retrospective studies report a small possible increased NAION risk with GLP-1 RAs such as semaglutide.
Some studies report no correlation between GLP-1 RAs and NAION.
Overall absolute risk of NAION remains low across the available literature.
Shared decision-making between patient and care team is recommended regarding GLP-1 RA use.
entries.limitations: Underlying evidence base relies primarily on retrospective observational studies using electronic health records and claims data, which are subject to significant confounding and bias.
entries.context: Appears consistent with emerging signals from recent observational studies raising concern about semaglutide and optic nerve ischemia; this consensus extends prior findings by synthesizing the evidence and providing formal clinical guidance from two major ophthalmology societies.

entries.title: MOTS-c is associated with oxidative stress and arterial stiffness in peritoneal dialysis patients: a pilot study.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c levels linked to oxidative stress and arterial stiffness in dialysis patients
entries.summary: This pilot study measured MOTS-c in serum, urine, and peritoneal dialysate of 32 stable peritoneal dialysis patients, finding that urinary MOTS-c inversely correlated with oxidative stress markers and dialysate MOTS-c inversely correlated with arterial stiffness and blood pressure, suggesting a novel mitochondrial-vascular axis in uremia.
keyFindings: Urinary MOTS-c inversely correlated with serum AOPPs (R = -0.592, p = 0.012), indicating lower oxidative stress with higher uMOTS-c.
Urinary MOTS-c positively associated with pulse wave velocity (R = 0.708, p = 0.001), possibly reflecting a compensatory vascular response.
Dialysate MOTS-c strongly and inversely correlated with PWV (R = -0.717, p = 0.019) and both systolic/diastolic blood pressure.
Urinary MOTS-c also positively associated with left ventricular systolic function (R = 0.440, p = 0.04).
entries.limitations: Small pilot sample of 32 patients limits statistical power and generalizability; cross-sectional design precludes causal inference about MOTS-c and cardiovascular outcomes.
entries.context: MOTS-c is a mitochondria-derived peptide previously implicated in metabolic homeostasis and vascular function, but its role in the uremic environment had not been explored. These findings extend prior mechanistic work by suggesting compartment-specific MOTS-c behavior in peritoneal dialysis patients.

entries.title: MOTS-c attenuates hyperoxia-induced neonatal cardiac injury by inhibiting oxeiptosis via maintaining the KEAP1-PGAM5 interaction.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c protects neonatal hearts from hyperoxia by blocking oxeiptosis pathway
entries.summary: This study examined whether MOTS-c, a mitochondrial-derived peptide, could protect neonatal mouse hearts from hyperoxia-induced injury. MOTS-c administration reduced cardiac hypertrophy, fibrosis, and dysfunction by maintaining the KEAP1-PGAM5 interaction and blocking AIFM1-mediated oxeiptosis.
keyFindings: Hyperoxia (85% O₂) caused significant cardiac hypertrophy, fibrosis, and dysfunction in neonatal mice with reduced serum MOTS-c levels.
MOTS-c administration markedly ameliorated hyperoxia-induced cardiac pathology and restored cardiac function in vivo.
MOTS-c inhibited the KEAP1-PGAM5-AIFM1 axis, preventing ROS-triggered oxeiptosis in cardiomyocytes.
KEAP1 overexpression abolished MOTS-c's protective effects, confirming KEAP1 as the critical mechanistic target.
entries.limitations: Study relies on neonatal mouse and H9C2 rat cell models only; translation to human neonates with hyperoxic cardiac injury remains undemonstrated.
entries.context: MOTS-c is known for anti-oxidative and cytoprotective effects in various tissues. This study extends prior findings by identifying oxeiptosis suppression via KEAP1-PGAM5 interaction as a novel cardioprotective mechanism, specifically in the neonatal hyperoxia context.

entries.title: Incretin-Based Anti-obesity Medications in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: The Evidence Map.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide and tirzepatide reviewed for PCOS treatment evidence
entries.summary: This narrative review maps evidence for liraglutide, semaglutide, and tirzepatide in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), evaluating mechanistic, clinical, and safety data. Semaglutide shows early weight-loss efficacy and preliminary signals for improved natural conception, while tirzepatide has no PCOS-specific evidence yet.
keyFindings: Semaglutide data are sparse but show weight-loss efficacy and preliminary signals of increased natural conception likelihood.
Tirzepatide has no PCOS-specific evidence and cannot be recommended beyond extrapolation from obesity/diabetes trials.
Liraglutide has the densest PCOS evidence, with visceral fat reduction, improved glycemia, and early androgen/fertility signals.
Reproductive safety, adolescent use, and long-term cardiometabolic outcomes remain major evidence gaps across all agents.
entries.limitations: Narrative review design with product-segmented framing; underlying studies cited are small, heterogeneous, and largely non-randomized, limiting strength of conclusions.
entries.context: Incretin-based therapies are increasingly explored for PCOS given the syndrome's links to obesity and insulin resistance. This review extends prior liraglutide-focused work by systematically mapping semaglutide and tirzepatide evidence, highlighting that PCOS-specific trial data remain critically limited.

entries.title: Semaglutide and Cardiovascular Outcomes in People with Type 2 Diabetes: A SUSTAIN-6 Post Hoc Analysis by Weight Loss Category.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide's cardioprotection appears weight-loss independent in T2D
entries.journal: Diabetes therapy : research, treatment and education of diabetes and related disorders
entries.summary: This post hoc analysis of the SUSTAIN-6 trial stratified 1,479 semaglutide-treated T2D patients by degree of weight loss over 56 weeks and found no significant difference in subsequent MACE rates across weight loss categories, suggesting semaglutide's cardiovascular benefits may operate through weight-independent mechanisms.
keyFindings: MACE incidence did not differ by weight loss category (P=0.73) across all four groups.
MACE event rates were similar: no loss/gain 1.05%, <5% loss 1.69%, 5–10% loss 1.06%, >10% loss 0.89%.
No degree of weight loss was associated with significantly lower adjusted odds of MACE vs. no weight loss.
Findings suggest semaglutide's cardioprotective effects may be mediated by weight-independent mechanisms.
entries.limitations: Post hoc, single-arm design limits causal inference; the placebo arm was excluded, and residual confounding cannot be ruled out despite age/sex adjustment.
entries.context: Semaglutide previously demonstrated cardiovascular benefit in SUSTAIN-6, but whether that benefit is driven by weight loss was unclear.
