These findings are consistent with emerging evidence that GLP-1RA cardioprotection may involve direct pleiotropic effects beyond weight reduction.

entries.title: LAT1-mediated delivery of engineered R13A-MOTS-c attenuates radiation-induced lung injury via Nrf2 activation and mitochondrial protection.
entries.displayTitle: Engineered MOTS-c analog reduces radiation lung injury via Nrf2 in mice
entries.summary: Researchers engineered R13A-MOTS-c, a hydrophobicity-enhanced analog of MOTS-c, to improve cellular uptake via LAT1 transporter. In mice exposed to 20 Gy thoracic irradiation, daily 5 mg/kg treatment for 2 weeks reduced pulmonary inflammation, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction through Nrf2 pathway activation.
keyFindings: Arginine-to-alanine substitution at position 13 raised the hydrophobicity index from -0.938 to -0.544, improving cellular uptake.
R13A-MOTS-c enters cells via LAT1-mediated transport, confirmed by competition assays and LAT1 knockdown.
In vivo, 5 mg/kg R13A-MOTS-c for 2 weeks mitigated radiation-induced lung inflammation and mitochondrial dysfunction in mice.
Protective effects were abolished by LAT1 inhibition, Nrf2 inhibition, or in Nrf2-knockout conditions, confirming the mechanism.
entries.limitations: Results are limited to mouse and cell-line models; no pharmacokinetic profiling or toxicity data for R13A-MOTS-c are reported, and clinical translatability remains undemonstrated.
entries.context: Wild-type MOTS-c has established antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties but limited membrane permeability. This study extends prior findings by engineering a modified analog with improved bioavailability and demonstrating in vivo efficacy in a radiation injury model.

entries.title: Effects of Semaglutide on Weight and Insulin Requirements in Two Adolescents With Type 1 Diabetes.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide improves glycemia and weight in two obese adolescents with T1D
entries.summary: Two female adolescents (ages 12 and 17) with type 1 diabetes and obesity were treated with once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide added to insulin pump therapy. After 12 months, both showed notable improvements in glycemic control, body weight, and insulin requirements with no adverse events.
keyFindings: Both adolescents achieved improved glycemic control after 12 months of adjunctive semaglutide therapy.
Weight reduction and decreased insulin requirements were observed in both patients.
No adverse events were reported over the 12-month treatment period.
Semaglutide was added to existing automated insulin delivery systems, not used as monotherapy.
entries.limitations: Only two patients reported with no control group; long-term effects on growth and body composition in adolescents remain unknown.
entries.context: GLP-1 receptor agonists are increasingly explored as adjuncts to insulin in type 1 diabetes; this case report extends limited pediatric evidence, suggesting semaglutide may offer metabolic benefits in adolescents with T1D and obesity, consistent with findings in adult T1D populations.

entries.title: Adia Med of Winter Park LLC Chronic Kidney Disease Research Study
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 trial tests glutathione plus stem cell therapy for CKD
entries.authors: Adia Med of Winter Park LLC
entries.journal: ClinicalTrials.gov · Adia Med of Winter Park LLC
entries.summary: This randomized, single-blind, placebo-controlled Phase 2 trial evaluates AdiaVita (umbilical cord blood-derived stem cells and exosomes) combined with intravenous and transdermal glutathione in ~100 adults with stage 2–4 chronic kidney disease, measuring changes in eGFR and serum creatinine over 12 months.
keyFindings: Phase 2 RCT; n=100 adults aged 18–80 with CKD stage 2–4 (eGFR 15–89 mL/min/1.73m²).
Three arms: AdiaVita + glutathione vs. glutathione + placebo vs. double placebo.
Primary endpoints: change in eGFR and serum creatinine at 12 months.
Monthly IV infusions for 3 months plus twice-daily transdermal glutathione spray; crossover option after month 3.
entries.limitations: Trial is not yet recruiting and has no posted results; efficacy and safety of this combination remain unestablished.
entries.context: Glutathione's antioxidant and cytoprotective properties have generated interest in CKD, where oxidative stress drives disease progression. This is among the first trials combining glutathione with allogeneic stem cell/exosome therapy for renal function improvement.

trialMeta.sponsor: Adia Med of Winter Park LLC
trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Change in Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR); Change in Serum Creatinine

entries.title: The GHK-Cu delays aging in Caenorhabditis elegans via coordinated regulation of mitochondrial function and activation of DAF-16/SKN-1 pathways.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu extends lifespan and delays aging in C. elegans
entries.summary: This study examined GHK-Cu (glycine-histidine-lysine complexed with copper) in C. elegans, finding it significantly extended lifespan, improved multiple aging-related phenotypes, preserved mitochondrial function, and activated DAF-16 and SKN-1 longevity pathways.
keyFindings: GHK-Cu significantly extended C. elegans lifespan and reduced lipofuscin and lipid accumulation.
GHK-Cu preserved mitochondrial membrane potential, reduced fragmentation, and promoted ATP biosynthesis.
Mitochondrial dynamics shifted toward fusion via regulation of drp-1 and fzo-1 gene expression.
GHK-Cu activated DAF-16 and SKN-1 pathways, upregulating antioxidant genes sod-3, gst-4, gcs-1, lys-7, and lys-8.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to an invertebrate model (C. elegans), and translation to mammalian aging biology remains to be demonstrated.
entries.context: GHK-Cu is known for antioxidant and tissue-repair properties in mammalian models. This study extends prior findings by providing the first mechanistic evidence linking GHK-Cu to lifespan extension via mitochondrial dynamics and conserved longevity transcription factors DAF-16 and SKN-1.

entries.title: Adia MED of Winter Park LLC Autism Spectrum Disorder Research Study
entries.displayTitle: Phase 1/2 trial tests glutathione plus stem cells for autism in children
entries.authors: Adia Med of Winter Park LLC
entries.journal: ClinicalTrials.gov · Adia Med of Winter Park LLC
entries.summary: This 24-month randomized trial in 100 children aged 3–12 with confirmed ASD compares glutathione alone versus glutathione combined with monthly IV umbilical cord blood-derived stem cell and exosome infusions (AdiaVita), measuring autism symptom change via the ATEC scale.
keyFindings: Phase 1/2 RCT with crossover design; n=100 children ages 3–12 with DSM-5 confirmed ASD.
Primary endpoint: change in total ATEC score at Month 6; 24-month follow-up total.
Glutathione delivered IV monthly plus twice-daily topical cream; AdiaVita arm adds monthly IV stem cell/exosome infusions.
Families pay $12,000 participation fee; no results posted; trial is active but not recruiting.
entries.limitations: No results have been posted; the pay-to-participate model and single-sponsor design raise significant methodological and ethical concerns that limit generalizability.
entries.context: Glutathione has been investigated as an antioxidant intervention in ASD based on evidence of oxidative stress in affected children. Combining it with stem cell/exosome therapy is experimental with no established efficacy data; this appears to be one of the first registered trials of this combination.

trialMeta.sponsor: Adia Med of Winter Park LLC
trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Change from Baseline in Total Autism Treatment Evaluation Checklist (ATEC) Score at Month 6

entries.title: Unexplained hypercapnia with normal pulmonary evaluation in a patient receiving semaglutide: a diagnostic challenge.
entries.displayTitle: Case report: rare central hypercapnia with semaglutide dose escalation
entries.summary: A 40-year-old patient developed unexplained respiratory acidosis and central hypercapnia four weeks after escalating semaglutide to 1 mg weekly. Symptoms and blood gas abnormalities resolved completely within three weeks of discontinuation.
keyFindings: Respiratory acidosis (pH 7.33, PaCO₂ 56 mmHg) developed 4 weeks after semaglutide dose escalation to 1 mg weekly
Nocturnal capnography showed sustained hypercapnia without apneic events (AHI <5), suggesting impaired central ventilatory drive
Complete symptom resolution within 10 days and normalized blood gases within 3 weeks after semaglutide discontinuation
Patient remained asymptomatic at 3-month follow-up after drug withdrawal
entries.limitations: Single case report cannot establish causation; temporal association and resolution after discontinuation are suggestive but not definitive proof of drug-related effect.
entries.context: This appears to be the first documented case of central respiratory depression associated with semaglutide. While GLP-1 receptor agonists are widely prescribed for obesity management, respiratory complications have rarely been reported in the literature.

entries.title: Injectable Peptides in Sports Medicine: A Structured Narrative Review of Evidence, Safety, and Antidoping Implications.
entries.displayTitle: Injectable peptides in sports medicine: mostly experimental, one class with solid evidence
entries.summary: This structured narrative review (2020–2025) examined injectable peptides relevant to orthopaedics and sports medicine across five functional classes, finding that GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide are the only class with reproducible randomized evidence of benefit, while growth hormone secretagogues and regenerative peptides remain investigational with unresolved safety and antidoping concerns.
keyFindings: Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) is the only injectable peptide class with reproducible RCT evidence for knee osteoarthritis symptom improvement, primarily via weight loss.
Structural cartilage modification from any injectable peptide class remains unproven in human studies.
CJC-1295, ipamorelin, and tesamorelin remain investigational with uncertain safety profiles and widespread antidoping restrictions.
Collagen-derived injectables showed only preliminary postoperative benefits in small, single-center prospective studies.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review (Level V evidence), it is subject to selection bias and does not include a formal meta-analysis or systematic synthesis of effect sizes.
entries.context: This review is consistent with the broader understanding that GLP-1 agonists have the strongest clinical evidence base among injectable peptides, while growth hormone axis secretagogues like CJC-1295 and ipamorelin are widely used off-label despite limited human trial data. It extends prior findings by explicitly contextualizing antidoping and product quality risks for sports medicine clinicians.

entries.title: In Vitro Metabolism and Analytical Characterization of SLU-PP-332 and SLU-PP-915: Novel Pan-ERR Agonists With Doping Potential.
entries.displayTitle: SLU-PP-332 metabolites mapped for sports doping detection
entries.journal: Rapid communications in mass spectrometry : RCM
entries.summary: Researchers characterized the in vitro metabolism of two pan-ERR agonists, SLU-PP-332 and SLU-PP-915, using human liver fractions and LC-HRMS/MS to support anti-doping testing. Nine metabolites were identified for SLU-PP-332 and seven for SLU-PP-915.
keyFindings: SLU-PP-332 produced 9 metabolites: 6 Phase-I and 3 Phase-II conjugates in human liver S9/microsome systems.
SLU-PP-915 yielded 7 Phase-I metabolites only, with no Phase-II conjugates detected.
Three SLU-PP-915 metabolites were confirmed via chemical synthesis and NMR spectroscopy.
Findings aim to enable detection of illicit use of these exercise-mimetic compounds in sports drug testing.
entries.limitations: Study is limited to in vitro metabolic models (S9 fraction and microsomes); in vivo metabolism and actual pharmacokinetics in humans remain uncharacterized.
entries.context: SLU-PP-332 and SLU-PP-915 are emerging exercise mimetics targeting estrogen-related receptors; this metabolic profiling work extends early pharmacological characterization toward anti-doping surveillance, consistent with growing interest in ERR agonists as performance-enhancing agents.

entries.title: Copper(II)-Tripeptide Complexes as Potential Skin Healing Agents: Synthesis, Characterization, and Wound Repair Ability.
entries.displayTitle: Novel copper-tripeptide complexes show wound-healing activity in cell assay
entries.summary: Researchers synthesized and characterized four copper(II)-tripeptide complexes (CuGGI, CuAAA, CuFGG, CuVYV) and tested their wound-healing potential in a scratch assay using MRC-5 fibroblast cells. CuAAA and CuVYV promoted cell migration and showed no cytotoxicity at effective doses.
keyFindings: CuAAA and CuVYV promoted wound closure by inducing cell migration in MRC-5 fibroblasts.
All four complexes were redox-active; CuVYV showed the most accessible Cu(III)/Cu(II) transition.
CuVYV formed a 1:1 complex via double deprotonation; CuGGI, CuAAA, CuFGG formed 1:2 complexes.
None of the compounds were cytotoxic at wound-repair-effective doses.
entries.limitations: Results are limited to a single in vitro scratch assay model; no animal or ex vivo skin data are presented, limiting translational conclusions.
entries.context: GHK-Cu is the best-known copper-tripeptide complex with established skin-repair and anti-inflammatory activity. This study extends the concept to novel tripeptide ligands, consistent with prior findings that copper coordination enhances peptide wound-healing properties.

entries.title: GLP-1 agonists and changes in body mass and composition in adults with overweight or obesity with or without type 2 diabetes mellitus: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists reduce body weight up to 9% primarily through fat loss
entries.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 36 studies found that GLP-1 receptor agonists consistently reduced body weight, BMI, and waist circumference in adults with overweight or obesity. Weight loss at 3 months averaged 9%, with predominant fat mass reduction and relative preservation of lean tissue.
keyFindings: At 3 months, mean body weight decreased by approximately 9% with marked reductions in fat mass and visceral adipose tissue
At 6 months, weight reduction averaged 5% with semaglutide, liraglutide, and exenatide showing comparable effects
At 12 months, weight loss persisted at around 4% with variability between agents
Fat mass decline predominated across all time points while lean body mass remained largely preserved
entries.limitations: Heterogeneity between studies and variability in outcome measurement methods; subgroup analyses by drug type may be limited by different study populations and protocols.
entries.context: This meta-analysis confirms GLP-1 agonists' established efficacy for weight loss and extends prior findings by quantifying body composition changes across multiple agents and timepoints. The preferential fat loss with lean mass preservation supports the metabolic benefits observed in prior trials.

entries.title: Cardiovascular outcomes of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists: A systematic review.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists reduce major cardiovascular events across multiple patient populations
entries.journal: American journal of health-system pharmacy : AJHP : official journal of the American Society of Health-System Pharmacists
entries.summary: Systematic review of 12 cardiovascular outcome trials found GLP-1 receptor agonists consistently reduce major adverse cardiovascular events in type 2 diabetes patients, with emerging benefits in obesity/atherosclerotic disease and heart failure populations.
keyFindings: GLP-1 agonists showed consistent MACE reduction in type 2 diabetes patients across 10 trials
Semaglutide lowered MACE risk in overweight/obesity patients with atherosclerotic disease but without diabetes
Tirzepatide reduced heart failure events and cardiovascular death in obesity with HFpEF patients
Gastrointestinal adverse events commonly led to treatment discontinuation across trials
entries.limitations: Effect on cardiovascular and all-cause mortality remains uncertain except for liraglutide; heterogeneous patient populations and follow-up durations across included trials.
entries.context: This review consolidates cardiovascular outcome data for GLP-1 receptor agonists, extending evidence beyond their established benefits in type 2 diabetes to include cardiovascular protection in non-diabetic obesity and heart failure populations.

entries.title: Real-World Comparison of Short-Term Adverse Events, Treatment Persistence, and Efficacy of Semaglutide and Tirzepatide: A Nationwide Multicenter Study.
entries.displayTitle: Tirzepatide achieves greater weight loss than semaglutide in real-world obesity treatment
entries.summary: This nationwide multicenter study of 2,549 patients with obesity compared semaglutide and tirzepatide over 6 months. Both showed similar overall adverse event rates (~51%), but tirzepatide produced greater weight loss (14.4% vs 12.6%).
keyFindings: Median weight loss at 6 months: 14.4% with tirzepatide vs 12.6% with semaglutide
Overall adverse event rates similar (~51% both groups), predominantly gastrointestinal
Tirzepatide showed higher rates of musculoskeletal and allergic reactions
Treatment discontinuation rates comparable except for pancreatic events (more with semaglutide)
entries.limitations: Observational design without randomization; short 6-month follow-up period limits assessment of long-term safety and durability of weight loss.
entries.context: This large real-world study confirms trial findings that tirzepatide produces modestly greater weight loss than semaglutide. The similar tolerability profiles and treatment persistence rates support both as effective obesity treatments, extending randomized trial data to routine clinical practice.

entries.title: Efficacy and safety of semaglutide injection in Indian patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus inadequately controlled on metformin: a phase 3, randomized, active-controlled trial (SIZE-DM study).
entries.displayTitle: Generic semaglutide matches branded version for glucose control in Indian T2DM patients
entries.summary: This phase 3 trial in 320 Indian adults with type 2 diabetes on metformin compared generic versus branded semaglutide over 24 weeks. Both achieved identical 2.2% HbA1c reductions with similar safety profiles, establishing non-inferiority of the generic formulation.
keyFindings: Both generic and branded semaglutide reduced HbA1c by mean 2.20% at 24 weeks
86.62% of participants achieved target HbA1c below 7.0%
Body weight and glucose reductions were comparable between treatment arms
Safety profiles similar with predominantly mild-to-moderate adverse events; no treatment-related serious adverse events
entries.limitations: Twenty-four-week duration may not capture long-term cardiovascular or renal outcomes that have been demonstrated with branded semaglutide in dedicated outcomes trials.
entries.context: This non-inferiority trial extends semaglutide evidence to Indian populations and establishes bioequivalence for a generic formulation. The efficacy findings are consistent with prior semaglutide trials showing robust HbA1c reductions of 1.5–2.5%, supporting access to GLP-1 therapy in resource-limited settings.

entries.title: Glucagon-like peptide-1 and atrial fibrillation incidence: Associations independent of weight loss.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 therapy cuts atrial fibrillation risk 33% independent of weight loss
entries.summary: A retrospective cohort study of 12,812 patients found GLP-1 receptor agonists reduced atrial fibrillation incidence by 33% and mortality by 66% compared to matched controls, with benefits persisting even in patients who gained weight, suggesting antiarrhythmic effects independent of metabolic changes.
keyFindings: GLP-1 use reduced AF risk by 33% (HR 0.67) and mortality by 66% (HR 0.34) versus controls
AF protection persisted across all weight change groups, including patients who gained weight
Only semaglutide showed statistically significant AF risk reduction among GLP-1 agents tested
AF risk reduction appeared after 24 months of continuous GLP-1 use
entries.limitations: Single-center retrospective design with AF ascertainment via manual chart review; residual confounding possible despite propensity matching.
entries.context: Prior studies have linked GLP-1 therapy to reduced AF incidence, but effects were assumed to be mediated primarily through weight loss. This study extends those findings by demonstrating AF protection independent of weight changes, suggesting direct antiarrhythmic mechanisms beyond metabolic effects.

entries.title: Efficacy, Safety and PK of Once-Daily Oral Semaglutide 25 mg for Obesity With and Without Type 2 Diabetes in Comparison With Subcutaneous Semaglutide 2.4 mg: A Model-Informed Drug Development Approach.
entries.displayTitle: Oral semaglutide 25 mg matches subcutaneous 2.4 mg exposure and weight loss
entries.summary: Model-informed drug development analysis compared once-daily oral semaglutide 25 mg to once-weekly subcutaneous 2.4 mg using pharmacokinetic modeling and simulated trials. Oral formulation achieved similar exposure levels and predicted weight reductions of 7.8–16.3% depending on diabetes status.
keyFindings: 82.2% of participants on oral 25 mg achieved exposure levels matching subcutaneous 2.4 mg formulation
Simulated weight reduction with oral 25 mg: 9.4% in people with T2D, 16.3% without T2D
Exposure-response modeling supported regulatory approval without direct comparative trial
entries.limitations: Efficacy estimates derived from simulated rather than actual trials; no direct comparative study between oral 25 mg and subcutaneous 2.4 mg formulations was conducted.
entries.context: This extends semaglutide's approved formulations by establishing bioequivalence between high-dose oral and subcutaneous formulations through modeling rather than direct head-to-head trials, consistent with established exposure-response relationships for GLP-1 receptor agonists.

entries.title: LEAN mass Preservation with Resistance Exercise and Protein during semaglutide and tirzepatide therapy (LEAN-PREP study): a protocol for a randomised controlled trial.
entries.displayTitle: Trial to test resistance training and protein for muscle preservation during GLP-1 therapy
entries.summary: The LEAN-PREP trial will randomize 232 adults with obesity in Kuwait to resistance exercise, protein supplementation, both, or neither alongside semaglutide or tirzepatide treatment to determine whether these interventions preserve muscle mass over 6 months.
keyFindings: 232 participants will be randomized 1:1:1:1 to control, exercise, protein, or combined interventions with GLP-1 therapy
Primary outcome is quadriceps cross-sectional area measured by MRI at 6 months
Interventions include home-based resistance training 3×/week and protein supplementation targeting 1.6 g/kg/day
Secondary outcomes include liver fat, body composition, strength, physical function, and metabolic markers
entries.limitations: This is a protocol publication describing planned methods; no efficacy or safety data are yet available from the trial itself.
entries.context: GLP-1 receptor agonists produce substantial weight loss but also significant lean mass reduction, raising sarcopenia concerns. This trial addresses a recognized clinical need to preserve muscle during pharmacological weight loss, particularly relevant in Kuwait where muscle weakness prevalence is high in people with type 2 diabetes.

entries.title: Thymosin Alpha 1 to Prevent COVID-19 Infection in Renal Dialysis Patients
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 thymosin alpha-1 COVID-19 prevention trial in dialysis patients posts results
entries.summary: This Phase 2 RCT tested whether thymosin alpha-1 (ZADAXIN) could prevent COVID-19 infection in 189 hemodialysis patients at high risk. The trial has completed and results are now posted on ClinicalTrials.gov.
keyFindings: Phase 2, n=189 hemodialysis patients, completed with results posted
Intervention: thymosin alpha-1 (28-amino acid synthetic peptide) vs. no treatment
Primary endpoint: number of participants with COVID-19 infection
Target population: end-stage renal disease patients on chronic hemodialysis
entries.limitations: Results are posted on registry but not yet peer-reviewed. Trial was conducted in a specific high-risk population, limiting generalizability.
entries.context: Thymosin alpha-1 is an immune modulator previously studied in SARS, sepsis, and hepatitis B. This trial tested its prophylactic use in a vulnerable population during the COVID-19 pandemic.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Number of Participants With COVID-19 Infection

entries.title: Therapeutics for Moderate and Severe Dengue
entries.displayTitle: Phase 3 platform trial tests N-acetylcysteine and dexamethasone for severe dengue
entries.authors: Oxford University Clinical Research Unit, Vietnam
entries.journal: ClinicalTrials.gov · Oxford University Clinical Research Unit, Vietnam
entries.summary: This large factorial trial will evaluate host-directed therapies in 8,800 patients hospitalized with moderate-to-severe dengue. Participants may receive N-acetylcysteine (a glutathione precursor), dexamethasone, or matched controls, with primary endpoints of progression to severe disease and 30-day mortality.
keyFindings: Phase 3, n=8,800, factorial design allowing multiple intervention comparisons within one trial
N-acetylcysteine given as 100 mg/kg/day continuous infusion for 4 days to protect liver function
Dexamethasone dose-adjusted by age/weight, 4-day course to modulate inflammatory response
Primary endpoints: progression to severe/critical dengue and all-cause mortality at 30 days
entries.limitations: Trial has not yet begun recruitment; results will not be available for several years. Efficacy and safety remain unproven.
entries.context: N-acetylcysteine is a glutathione precursor typically used for acetaminophen overdose; this trial tests whether antioxidant protection can prevent dengue-related liver injury and vascular leak. Prior small studies suggested benefit, but large-scale evidence in dengue is lacking.

trialMeta.sponsor: Oxford University Clinical Research Unit, Vietnam
trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Progression to severe dengue/critical dengue; All-cause mortality within 30 days

entries.title: Carbonless amino acids and a carbonless GHK peptide.
entries.displayTitle: Computational study replaces carbon atoms with boron-nitrogen in GHK peptide
entries.summary: Computational chemistry calculations explored replacing all carbon atoms in GHK peptide with boron-nitrogen pairs. The carbonless analogue (cGHK) showed greater conformational flexibility and bound Cu(II) 6.24 kcal/mol more strongly than natural GHK under aqueous conditions.
keyFindings: Carbonless GHK (cGHK) exhibited broader conformational landscape than natural GHK in aqueous conditions
cGHK bound Cu(II) with 6.24 kcal/mol stronger stabilization than natural GHK at 298 K
DFT calculations identified unique low-energy carbonless analogues of glycine, histidine, and lysine
entries.limitations: Purely computational study with no experimental synthesis or biological validation of the proposed carbonless peptide structures.
entries.context: GHK is known for copper-binding properties important in wound healing and tissue repair. This theoretical study explores whether carbon-free molecular scaffolds could retain or enhance these binding characteristics through systematic boron-nitrogen substitution.

entries.title: Glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine-Cu2(GHK-Cu) Attenuates CuSOor LPS induced-inflammation in Zebrafish larvae model.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu suppresses inflammation and oxidative stress in zebrafish larvae
entries.summary: GHK-Cu was tested in zebrafish larvae exposed to copper sulfate or LPS. The peptide reduced immune cell migration, decreased pro-inflammatory cytokines, lowered oxidative stress markers, and downregulated the JAK1 pathway.
keyFindings: GHK-Cu decreased neutrophil and macrophage migration to inflammation sites
Suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and increased anti-inflammatory IL-10
Reduced nitric oxide and reactive oxygen species while improving SOD activity
Downregulated JAK1 signaling pathway
entries.limitations: Zebrafish larvae model may not fully recapitulate mammalian inflammatory responses; no mammalian validation provided.
entries.context: GHK-Cu is widely used in cosmetics for anti-aging and tissue repair, but its anti-inflammatory mechanisms have been less characterized. This study extends prior findings by demonstrating anti-inflammatory effects through cytokine modulation and JAK1 pathway inhibition in an in vivo model.

entries.title: Safety and Efficacy of Approved and Unapproved Peptide Therapies for Musculoskeletal Injuries and Athletic Performance.
entries.displayTitle: Review examines safety gaps in approved and unapproved peptides for sports injuries
entries.summary: Narrative review of peptides marketed for musculoskeletal injury recovery and athletic performance, covering pharmacological mechanisms, safety profiles, and regulatory status. Authors emphasize that many unapproved peptides show promise in animal models but lack rigorous human safety data.
keyFindings: Many unapproved peptides demonstrate favorable tissue repair and metabolic outcomes in animal models
Rigorous human safety data are scarce for unapproved peptides, with potential for serious patient harm
A 'gray market' of unapproved compounds operates largely outside regulatory oversight
Social media amplifies placebo effects associated with peptide use
entries.limitations: As a narrative rather than systematic review, study selection may not be exhaustive and synthesis is subject to author interpretation.
entries.context: This review synthesizes current knowledge on peptides increasingly used in sports medicine, highlighting the gap between animal research and human clinical evidence. It addresses the growing direct-to-consumer market for peptides that lack formal regulatory approval.

entries.title: Monitoring Patients With Repetitive Head Impact With Gamma-glutamylcysteine Supplementation
entries.displayTitle: Phase 1 trial tests gamma-glutamylcysteine precursor for brain glutathione in repetitive head injury
entries.summary: This 30-participant trial evaluates whether oral gamma-glutamylcysteine (400mg twice daily) can increase brain and blood glutathione levels in retired athletes and military personnel with repetitive head impacts, measured by MR spectroscopy and cognitive testing.
keyFindings: Phase 1 trial in 30 retired NFL players and Navy SEALs with repetitive head impact history
Intervention: 800mg/day oral gamma-glutamylcysteine, a glutathione precursor peptide
Primary endpoints: brain glutathione by MR spectroscopy and blood glutathione levels
Currently recruiting; no results posted yet
entries.limitations: This is an early-stage trial registration with no published results; efficacy of gamma-glutamylcysteine for neuroprotection remains unproven.
entries.context: Glutathione is the brain's primary antioxidant and cannot cross the blood-brain barrier directly. This trial tests whether gamma-glutamylcysteine, a precursor dipeptide, can increase brain glutathione to address oxidative stress from repetitive head trauma.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Changes in brain glutathione levels (mM) in people with Repetitive Head Injury (RHI) using Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy in pre and post supplementation.; Changes in baseline blood glutathione levels (µmol/l) in people with RHI due to GGC supplementation.

entries.title: Enrichment of Glutathione Using Gamma-glutamylcysteine Supplementation in Parkinson's Disease Patients.
entries.displayTitle: Phase 1 trial tests gamma-glutamylcysteine supplementation to boost brain glutathione in Parkinson's
entries.summary: This first-in-human trial examines whether oral gamma-glutamylcysteine (GGC) supplementation can raise brain and blood glutathione levels in early Parkinson's disease patients over 12 months, measured by MR spectroscopy, with secondary assessments of motor function, cognition, and gut microbiome.
keyFindings: Phase 1, n=12 early PD patients, 400 mg GGC twice daily for 12 months
Primary endpoint: brain glutathione levels (mM) via MR spectroscopy at baseline vs. 12 months
Secondary endpoints include brain iron, motor function, cognitive skills, and gut microbiome changes
Currently recruiting; no results posted yet
entries.limitations: Small pilot study (n=12) with no results yet. Trial registration does not demonstrate efficacy; MR spectroscopy can measure glutathione but clinical benefit remains unproven.
entries.context: Glutathione depletion is implicated in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology. This trial tests a precursor strategy to directly raise brain glutathione, an approach previously explored with N-acetylcysteine. GGC is a more direct glutathione precursor than NAC, potentially offering greater CNS bioavailability.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Changes in brain glutathione levels (mM) in people with Parkinson's Disease using Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy compared to post-supplementation with GGC.; Changes in brain iron levels (ppb) in people with Parkinson's Disease using Magnetic Resonance Imaging between pre and post GGC supplementation.

entries.title: Are serum MOTS-c levels and MOTS-c m.1382A>C polymorphism related to polycystic ovary syndrome?
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c levels similar in adolescents with and without PCOS
entries.summary: This case-control study compared serum MOTS-c levels and m.1382A>C polymorphism in 121 adolescents with PCOS versus 125 healthy controls. MOTS-c levels were slightly but not significantly elevated in PCOS, and all participants had wild-type genotype.
keyFindings: Mean MOTS-c levels higher in PCOS group but difference not statistically significant (p=0.059)
No significant association between MOTS-c levels and anthropometric or metabolic parameters in PCOS group
All 246 participants had wild-type (A/A) genotype for m.1382A>C polymorphism
Findings suggest MOTS-c may have minor involvement in PCOS pathophysiology
entries.limitations: All participants had the same genotype, preventing analysis of polymorphism effects; trend toward higher MOTS-c in PCOS (p=0.059) may warrant larger studies.
entries.context: MOTS-c is known to improve insulin sensitivity and metabolic function. This study extends MOTS-c research to adolescent PCOS, a condition associated with insulin resistance, but found no significant differences in peptide levels or genetic polymorphism between affected and healthy adolescents.

entries.title: Comparative Proteomic Analysis of the Secretome of Control and BRAF/MEK Inhibitor-Resistant Melanoma Cells.
entries.displayTitle: Melanoma cells resistant to BRAF/MEK inhibitors secrete thymosin beta-4
entries.summary: Proteomic analysis of melanoma cells resistant to vemurafenib and cobimetinib identified thymosin beta-4 among proteins secreted in significantly higher amounts compared to non-resistant cells, suggesting potential roles in drug resistance and tumor microenvironment modification.
keyFindings: Thymosin beta-4 secretion significantly elevated in BRAF/MEK inhibitor-resistant melanoma cells versus controls
Resistant cells' secretome enriched in proteins related to cancer progression, adhesion, cytoskeleton, and matrix organization
Secreted proteins may promote pro-tumor niche formation in the microenvironment
Thymosin beta-4 identified as potential biomarker or therapeutic target in treatment-resistant melanoma
entries.limitations: Study limited to in vitro cell culture models; functional consequences of elevated thymosin beta-4 secretion and its specific role in resistance mechanisms were not directly tested.
entries.context: Thymosin beta-4 is known to regulate actin dynamics and has been implicated in cancer cell migration and metastasis. This study extends those findings by linking elevated TB-4 secretion specifically to acquired resistance against targeted melanoma therapies.

entries.title: Thymosin α1 improves the outcomes of patients with hepatitis B virus-related acute-on-chronic liver failure by restoring immune balance.
entries.displayTitle: Thymosin alpha-1 boosts 90-day survival in HBV liver failure by rebalancing immunity
entries.summary: Open-label RCT in 73 HBV-related acute-on-chronic liver failure patients found thymosin alpha-1 plus standard therapy increased 90-day transplant-free survival by reducing regulatory T cells and moderating late-stage hyperinflammation without suppressing early immune activation.
keyFindings: Thymosin alpha-1 significantly increased 90-day transplant-free survival versus standard therapy alone
Treatment reduced regulatory T cell frequencies at weeks 4-8, particularly CD226+ Treg subsets
Moderated late-stage hyperinflammatory response while preserving early immune activation
Survivors showed higher baseline effector T cells and pro-inflammatory cytokines than non-survivors
entries.limitations: Open-label design without blinding may introduce bias; mechanism-of-action endpoints were exploratory rather than pre-specified primary outcomes.
entries.context: Thymosin alpha-1 is known to have immunomodulatory effects in severe infection and liver disease. This study extends prior survival observations by demonstrating specific T-cell and cytokine mechanisms—particularly Treg reduction and inflammation moderation—that appear to underlie the clinical benefit in HBV-related acute-on-chronic liver failure.

entries.title: MOTS-c attenuates cardiac dysfunction following high altitude exposure by promoting mitophagy.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c rescues persistent cardiac dysfunction after high-altitude exposure via mitophagy
entries.summary: Mice exposed to simulated 6000-m altitude for 10 days showed persistent cardiac dysfunction during de-acclimatization at lower altitude. Exogenous MOTS-c supplementation restored cardiac function by activating the Pink1/Parkin mitophagy pathway and improving mitochondrial quality.
keyFindings: Cardiac dysfunction induced by 10-day high-altitude exposure persisted throughout 10-day de-acclimatization at lower altitude
MOTS-c supplementation during de-acclimatization alleviated cardiac dysfunction and mitochondrial dysfunction
MOTS-c promoted mitophagy via Pink1/Parkin pathway; silencing Pink1 abolished protective effects
Reduced circulating MOTS-c levels observed in patients with high altitude heart disease and acute coronary syndrome
entries.limitations: Animal model findings may not fully translate to human high-altitude cardiac pathology; optimal MOTS-c dosing and timing for human application remain unknown.
entries.context: MOTS-c is a mitochondrial-derived peptide previously shown to improve metabolic and mitochondrial function. This study extends those findings by demonstrating a specific role in cardiac de-acclimatization after hypoxic stress and identifies Pink1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy as the mechanism.

entries.title: MOTS-c for Improving Insulin Sensitivity in Adults With Prediabetes and Overweight/Obesity
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 MOTS-c trial for insulin sensitivity in prediabetes begins recruitment
entries.summary: Hudson Biotech is enrolling 120 adults with prediabetes and overweight/obesity into a 12-week randomized, placebo-controlled Phase 2 trial testing whether subcutaneous MOTS-c, a mitochondrial-derived peptide, improves insulin sensitivity measured by oral glucose tolerance testing.
keyFindings: Phase 2, randomized 1:1 to MOTS-c vs placebo, n=120, 12-week treatment period
Primary endpoint: change in OGTT-derived insulin sensitivity (Matsuda Index) from baseline
Secondary endpoints include HbA1c, fasting glucose, lipids, body weight, and waist circumference
Currently recruiting; results not yet available
entries.limitations: Trial is in active recruitment; no efficacy or safety data are yet available. Registry entry does not provide dosing details or planned injection frequency.
entries.context: MOTS-c is a mitochondrial-encoded peptide reported in preclinical models to activate AMPK in skeletal muscle and improve insulin sensitivity. This is the first registered human trial testing MOTS-c for metabolic endpoints in a prediabetes population.

trialMeta.primaryOutcomes: Change from baseline in OGTT-derived insulin sensitivity (Matsuda Index); Incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs)

entries.title: Kisspeptin-10 Promotes Hormone Secretion, Ovarian Follicles Development and Fecundity via PI3K/AKT/ERK Signal Pathway in Mice.
entries.displayTitle: Kisspeptin-10 boosts ovarian function and fertility via PI3K/AKT/ERK pathway in mice
entries.summary: Kisspeptin-10 treatment in mice enhanced follicular granulosa cell proliferation, reduced apoptosis and autophagy, promoted ovarian follicle development, and increased progesterone and estradiol secretion. Effects were mediated through PI3K/AKT/ERK signaling, with 10 μg/g showing optimal efficacy.
keyFindings: KP-10 increased granulosa cell proliferation while decreasing apoptosis and autophagy rates (p < 0.05)
Secondary follicle number, diameter, and wall thickness significantly increased with KP-10 treatment (p < 0.05)
Serum progesterone and estradiol concentrations were notably elevated versus control (p < 0.05)
Optimal dose of 10 μg/g KP-10 activated PI3K/AKT/ERK pathway genes and proteins
entries.limitations: Mouse model may not fully translate to human reproductive physiology; optimal dosing and long-term safety in humans remain unknown.
entries.context: Kisspeptin-10 is known to regulate reproductive hormone secretion through hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. This study extends prior findings by demonstrating direct ovarian effects via PI3K/AKT/ERK signaling in both cultured cells and intact animals.

entries.title: Kisspeptin Restores Placental mTOR Signaling and Improves Glucose Homeostasis Mediators Disrupted by Maternal Hypothyroidism in Rats.
entries.displayTitle: Kisspeptin-10 restores placental mTOR signaling disrupted by maternal hypothyroidism in rats
entries.summary: In pregnant rats with propylthiouracil-induced hypothyroidism, kisspeptin-10 treatment starting on gestational day 8 restored placental mTOR signaling and normalized glucose transporter expression, though it did not prevent fetal growth restriction or improve maternal glucose intolerance.
keyFindings: Maternal hypothyroidism reduced fetal/placental weights and caused glucose intolerance with decreased insulin and HDL levels
Kisspeptin-10 restored placental AKT/mTOR expression and attenuated Glut1 dysregulation caused by hypothyroidism
Kisspeptin-10 upregulated placental IGF1/IGF1R axis but did not prevent feto-placental growth restriction
Treatment improved placental molecular markers without reversing maternal glucose homeostasis deficits
entries.limitations: Kisspeptin-10 improved placental molecular markers but failed to prevent growth restriction or maternal metabolic dysfunction, indicating limited functional rescue in this model.
entries.context: This extends prior findings that kisspeptin can improve feto-placental development in hypothyroid rats. The restoration of placental mTOR signaling is notable given its known role in nutrient sensing and fetal growth, though the lack of phenotypic rescue suggests molecular changes alone may be insufficient.

entries.title: Integrated transcriptomics and miRNA-mRNA network analysis reveals Kisspeptin-10 mediated regulation of EMT and apoptosis in glioblastoma.
entries.displayTitle: Kisspeptin-10 modulates EMT and apoptosis pathways in glioblastoma cells
entries.summary: This study combined transcriptomic analysis of 1,401 differentially expressed genes with in-vitro experiments to show that Kisspeptin-10 regulates epithelial-mesenchymal transition and apoptosis in glioblastoma cells, identifying key hub genes and regulatory miRNAs involved in tumor progression.
keyFindings: 1,401 differentially expressed genes identified, with enrichment in EMT regulation, cell-cycle, and apoptosis pathways
Hub genes CDK1, CDC20, JUN, and FABP5 identified as key regulatory targets
miR-200, miR-345, and miR-577 emerged as regulatory miRNAs linked to EMT and apoptotic signaling
In-vitro validation confirmed Kisspeptin-10 modulates EMT and apoptosis markers in glioblastoma cells
entries.limitations: Study relies on publicly available datasets and in-vitro validation only; no animal models or clinical data to confirm therapeutic relevance in glioblastoma patients.
entries.context: Kisspeptin-10 is known as a metastasis-suppressor peptide in several cancers, but this is the first study to investigate its effects in glioblastoma. The findings are consistent with its established role in modulating EMT and apoptotic pathways in other tumor types.

entries.title: Cuproptosis Is Induced in Drug-Induced Liver Injury by Oxidative Stress-Mediated Copper Overload.
entries.displayTitle: Glutathione and Nrf2 activation reverse copper-driven liver injury in acetaminophen toxicity
entries.summary: Mouse and cell models of acetaminophen-induced liver injury revealed cuproptosis triggered by oxidative stress and copper overload. Glutathione supplementation and Nrf2 activation reversed cuproptosis, reduced copper accumulation, and ameliorated liver damage.
keyFindings: Acetaminophen overdose induced cuproptosis via decreased FDX1, lipoyl synthase, and DLAT levels alongside copper overload
Copper depletion via low-copper diet reversed cuproptosis and liver injury; high-copper diet exacerbated both
Glutathione supplementation and Nrf2 activation relieved oxidative stress, reduced copper accumulation, and reversed liver injury
FDX1 knockdown induced cuproptosis and worsened liver damage in mice and cells
entries.limitations: Study uses animal and cell models; clinical relevance of cuproptosis in human acetaminophen toxicity and optimal glutathione dosing remain to be established.
entries.context: This extends prior knowledge of glutathione's role in acetaminophen toxicity beyond its direct detoxification function, linking antioxidant effects to copper homeostasis. Nrf2 activation is a known therapeutic target in drug-induced liver injury; this study adds a cuproptosis-inhibition mechanism.

entries.title: Mitochondrial-derived peptide MOTS-c targets SLC7A11 to preserve spermatogenesis by suppressing ferroptosis.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c protects sperm production by blocking ferroptosis in human and mouse models
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse and human observational
entries.summary: Researchers found that MOTS-c, a mitochondrial peptide, was reduced in men with poor sperm quality and protected against stress-induced sperm production defects in mice by targeting SLC7A11 to suppress ferroptosis and oxidative stress.
keyFindings: MOTS-c levels were significantly lower in serum of men with oligoasthenozoospermia and correlated with semen quality parameters
Exogenous MOTS-c ameliorated microgravity-induced sperm defects by suppressing oxidative stress and ferroptosis
MOTS-c targets SLC7A11, a key anti-ferroptosis molecule, to protect spermatogonia proliferation
Loss- and gain-of-function studies confirmed SLC7A11 inhibits ferroptosis and promotes spermatogonia proliferation
entries.limitations: The microgravity mechanical stress model may not fully replicate common clinical causes of male infertility; human data limited to serum correlations with no interventional component.
entries.context: This extends prior knowledge that MOTS-c protects testicular function by identifying ferroptosis suppression via SLC7A11 as a specific mechanism. The clinical correlation with human semen parameters suggests potential translational relevance for male infertility.

entries.title: Intranasal Insulin and Glutathione as an Add-On Therapy in Parkinson's Disease
entries.displayTitle: Phase 2 intranasal insulin-glutathione trial in Parkinson's disease recruiting
entries.journal: ClinicalTrials.gov · Gateway Institute for Brain Research
entries.summary: A 56-patient Phase 2 randomized controlled trial testing intranasal insulin combined with glutathione administered twice daily versus matched placebo in Parkinson's disease patients, with verbal fluency as the primary outcome measure.
keyFindings: Phase 2 trial (n=56) testing intranasal insulin-glutathione combination versus placebo in Parkinson's disease
Primary endpoint is verbal fluency; currently recruiting participants
Requires stable PD medication regimen for 30 days; excludes diabetic patients
Intervention administered twice daily via intranasal route as add-on to standard therapy
entries.limitations: Trial is recruiting and results are not yet available; registration does not constitute evidence of efficacy.
entries.context: Intranasal insulin has shown neuroprotective potential in neurodegenerative diseases by improving brain insulin signaling. This trial tests whether combining insulin with antioxidant glutathione provides cognitive or motor benefits in Parkinson's disease as adjunct therapy.

entries.title: Mitochondria-derived peptide MOTS-c alleviates hyperoxia-induced bronchopulmonary dysplasia in neonatal mice by activating Nrf2 pathway.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c reduces lung damage in hyperoxia-induced bronchopulmonary dysplasia mice
entries.summary: MOTS-c, a 16-amino-acid mitochondrial peptide, was tested in neonatal mice with hyperoxia-induced bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD). Exogenous MOTS-c alleviated alveolar simplification, reduced inflammation and oxidative stress, and promoted vascular development through Nrf2 pathway activation.
keyFindings: MOTS-c supplementation attenuated alveolar simplification and pulmonary vascular abnormalities in 70% oxygen-exposed mice
MOTS-c reduced inflammation and oxidative stress both in vivo and in hyperoxia-stimulated HUVECs
Protective effects were Nrf2-dependent; benefits abolished by Nrf2 inhibitor ML385 or Nrf2 deficiency
MOTS-c levels were reduced in hyperoxia-induced BPD mice at baseline
entries.limitations: Animal model only; translation to human neonatal BPD uncertain given species differences in lung development and hyperoxia susceptibility.
entries.context: MOTS-c is known to modulate oxidative stress in metabolic and aging-related conditions. This study extends its application to neonatal lung injury, demonstrating Nrf2-mediated antioxidant effects in BPD, a major complication of prematurity with limited therapies.

entries.title: Therapeutic peptides in gerontology: mechanisms and applications for healthy aging.
entries.displayTitle: Narrative review maps nine peptides targeting key aging mechanisms
entries.summary: A narrative review of 20 sources examined nine therapeutic peptides (including tirzepatide, epitalon, GHK-Cu, BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, CJC-1295, ipamorelin, bremelanotide) addressing metabolic dysfunction, telomere biology, tissue repair, neuroprotection, and hormonal decline in aging populations.
keyFindings: Nine peptides identified spanning metabolic restoration, telomere biology, dermal regeneration, tissue repair, neuroprotection, and growth hormone modulation.
FDA-approved agents show robust safety from large trials; investigational peptides have promising preclinical evidence but lack long-term safety data.
Significant knowledge gaps include optimal dosing, combination therapy effects, and efficacy biomarkers.
Investigational peptides require rigorous clinical trials to establish safety and efficacy for healthspan extension.
entries.limitations: Narrative review of only 20 primary sources; lacks systematic search methodology, quality assessment, or meta-analysis typical of systematic reviews.
entries.context: This review synthesizes emerging evidence on peptides targeting aging hallmarks, consistent with growing interest in peptide therapeutics for gerontological applications. The distinction between FDA-approved agents with established safety profiles and investigational peptides requiring validation reflects the current regulatory and evidence landscape.

entries.title: Combined antiretroviral therapy with low- or normal-protein, high-calorie diets appears to induce significant deleterious electrocardiographic changes in a rodent model.
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin prevents cardiac damage from antiretroviral drugs in rats
entries.journal: Brazilian journal of medical and biological research = Revista brasileira de pesquisas medicas e biologicas
entries.summary: In Sprague Dawley rats fed calorie-dense diets, dolutegravir-based and classical antiretroviral regimens caused ECG abnormalities and myocardial fibrosis. Adding tesamorelin prevented these cardiac effects, suggesting growth hormone pathway involvement.
keyFindings: Dolutegravir and classical cART with calorie-dense diets caused significant ECG changes including altered wave amplitudes, intervals, and QTc (P<0.0001)
Myocardial fibrosis occurred in rats receiving antiretrovirals with low- or normal-protein calorie-dense diets (P<0.0001)
Tesamorelin co-administration prevented ECG abnormalities and myocardial fibrosis induced by dolutegravir
Findings implicate growth hormone pathway dysfunction in antiretroviral-associated cardiac pathology
entries.limitations: Rat model may not fully recapitulate human HIV/cART cardiotoxicity; relatively short 9-week treatment duration; mechanisms of tesamorelin's protective effect not fully elucidated.
entries.context: Tesamorelin is a growth hormone-releasing hormone analogue approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy. This study extends its potential cardioprotective role by demonstrating prevention of antiretroviral-induced myocardial changes, consistent with growth hormone's known metabolic and cardiovascular effects.
